summary: Autonomous medical and robotic systems increasingly rely on intelligent perception and reasoning capabilities to interpret visual data and support clinical decision making. Radiology report generation represents a critical component of such automated diagnostic workflows, yet existing end-to-end multimodal models often suffer from weak visual grounding, resulting in unreliable interpretations and omission of subtle clinical findings. This paper presents XMedFusion, a modular AI framework designed as an intelligent perception and reasoning module for autonomous medical systems. The proposed framework decomposes visual information into coordinated functional components that emulate expert-driven analysis, including a visual perception agent that extracts image-grounded evidence, a knowledge graph construction agent that structures clinically relevant findings, and a retrieval-guided drafting process that ensures a consistent reporting structure. A synthesis agent iteratively integrates visual and structured evidence through reasoning-driven verification to produce reliable and interpretable diagnostic outputs. Experimental evaluation on a public chest radiograph dataset demonstrates significant improvements over baseline vision-language models, achieving gains from 0.0493 to 0.3359 in BLEU-1, 0.0863 to 0.2440 in ROUGE-L, and 0.0829 to 0.1708 in METEOR, along with substantial improvements in semantic evaluation metrics such as Consistency (2.38 to 7.80) and Accuracy (2.34 to 6.93). The results highlight the effectiveness of structured multi-agent perception and reasoning for enhancing robustness, transparency, and automation in intelligent medical imaging systems, enabling integration into autonomous healthcare and robotic diagnostic workflows.

AI.tldr: XMedFusion is a modular AI framework for autonomous medical systems that decomposes radiology report generation into coordinated agents (visual perception, knowledge graph construction, retrieval-guided drafting, synthesis) to improve visual grounding and diagnostic accuracy, achieving significant gains over baseline models on chest radiograph datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing end-to-end multimodal models for radiology report generation suffer from weak visual grounding, unreliable interpretations, and omission of subtle clinical findings, limiting their deployment in autonomous medical and robotic systems.
AI.method: XMedFusion is a modular framework with four coordinated agents: (1) a visual perception agent for image-grounded evidence extraction, (2) a knowledge graph construction agent for structuring clinical findings, (3) a retrieval-guided drafting process for consistent report structure, and (4) a synthesis agent that iteratively integrates evidence through reasoning-driven verification.
AI.result: On a public chest radiograph dataset, XMedFusion outperformed baseline vision-language models with gains of 0.0493–0.3359 in BLEU-1, 0.0863–0.2440 in ROUGE-L, 0.0829–0.1708 in METEOR, and substantial improvements in semantic metrics: Consistency (2.38 to 7.80) and Accuracy (2.34 to 6.93).
AI.conclusion: The structured multi-agent perception and reasoning approach enhances robustness, transparency, and automation in intelligent medical imaging systems, enabling reliable integration into autonomous healthcare and robotic diagnostic workflows.

title: An Empirical Analysis of Optimization Dynamics and Sparsity Boundaries in Large-Scale Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
summary: Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR) is critical for video surveillance, enabling forensic search and re-identification systems. Extreme class imbalance remains a fundamental obstacle when merging PETA and PA-100K into a 109,000-image composite corpus, where minority attributes have positive sample fractions below 1%. This causes standard BCE optimization to suppress rare traits, a phenomenon we term the majority negative class cheating trap. We present a systematic ablation of Multi-Label Focal Loss hyperparameters (alpha and gamma) on a ResNet-18 backbone. A calibrated configuration (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) achieves a Macro F1-score of 62.32%, matching BCE baseline while preserving superior hard-example mining and convergence dynamics. Our approach uses pure loss-function engineering with zero computational overhead for edge deployment. We identify the Sparsity Wall, a hard boundary where positive sample fractions below 0.1% make global loss reweighting ineffective, requiring instance-level intervention.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles class imbalance in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition by applying Multi-Label Focal Loss to a merged 109K-image dataset. The calibrated loss (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) matches BCE performance (Macro F1=62.32%) with better hard-example mining and zero computational overhead. The paper also identifies the "Sparsity Wall" where global reweighting fails for extremely rare attributes (below 0.1% positives).
AI.motivation: Extreme class imbalance in PAR datasets (some attributes <1% positive samples) causes standard BCE optimization to suppress rare traits—a problem called the "majority negative class cheating trap." The authors aim to address this without adding computational overhead.
AI.method: Systematic ablation of Multi-Label Focal Loss hyperparameters (alpha and gamma) on a ResNet-18 backbone, using a composite 109K-image corpus merged from PETA and PA-100K. Pure loss-function engineering with no extra computation.
AI.result: Calibrated configuration (alpha=0.50, gamma=2.0) achieves Macro F1-score of 62.32%, matching the BCE baseline, while offering superior hard-example mining and convergence dynamics. Identifies the "Sparsity Wall" at <0.1% positive fraction where global reweighting fails.
AI.conclusion: Multi-Label Focal Loss with proper calibration can match BCE performance in imbalanced PAR while being computationally free. However, global loss reweighting has a hard limit (Sparsity Wall), necessitating instance-level interventions for extremely rare attributes.

title: ScoutVLA: UAV-Centric Active Perception via a Dual-Expert VLA Model for Open-World Embodied Question Answering
summary: Aerial Embodied Question Answering (EQA) requires Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to actively perceive the environment and answer natural language questions. Existing outdoor EQA systems usually stop once the target enters the UAV's field of view, leaving the fine-grained viewpoint adjustment needed for evidence-seeking questions largely unresolved. To address this issue, we introduce FG-EQA, a fine-grained active perception EQA benchmark with more than 40K simulated trajectories and 1K real-world trajectories. Drawing inspiration from the ``waggle dance'' of scout bees, which iteratively adjust their flight paths to verify target information, we propose ScoutVLA, an evidence-driven Vision-Language-Action model for outdoor EQA. To emulate this active exploration behavior, ScoutVLA features a decoupled dual-expert architecture: a vision-language expert infers the semantic intent to identify missing evidence, while an independent action expert employs high-DoF flow matching to generate continuous viewpoint-refinement trajectories. To balance the competing demands of continuous control and semantic reasoning, we devise a decoupled training strategy with a knowledge insulation mechanism that prevents the action gradients from erasing the model's multimodal reasoning ability. Extensive simulated experiments and a qualitative real-world field study both verify the superiority of ScoutVLA over the state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating a 10.48$\boldsymbol{\times}$ higher average strict success rate and a 7.72$\boldsymbol{\times}$ higher average QA correctness.

AI.tldr: FG-EQA is a fine-grained active perception benchmark for outdoor EQA with 40K+ simulated and 1K real trajectories. ScoutVLA, inspired by bees' waggle dance, uses a decoupled dual-expert architecture (vision-language + action) with knowledge insulation to perform evidence-driven viewpoint refinement, achieving 10.48× higher success rate and 7.72× higher QA correctness over SOTA.
AI.motivation: Existing outdoor EQA systems stop once the target enters the UAV's field of view, failing to perform fine-grained viewpoint adjustments needed for evidence-seeking questions. This gap motivates the need for active, iterative perception behavior in UAVs.
AI.method: Proposes ScoutVLA, an evidence-driven Vision-Language-Action model with (1) a decoupled dual-expert architecture: a vision-language expert for semantic intent inference and an action expert using high-DoF flow matching for continuous trajectory generation; (2) a decoupled training strategy with knowledge insulation to protect multimodal reasoning from action gradients.
AI.result: ScoutVLA achieves 10.48× higher average strict success rate and 7.72× higher average QA correctness over state-of-the-art baselines in simulation; validated qualitatively in real-world field study.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained active perception inspired by biological behavior (bee waggle dance) significantly improves EQA performance, and decoupled architecture with knowledge insulation effectively balances continuous control and semantic reasoning.

title: Double-Helix Vision (DH-V2): A Geometry-Based Visual Sampler for Bandwidth-Constrained Perception
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Code and benchmarks: https://github.com/JackJ-C/double-helix-vision-tool
summary: We present Double-Helix Vision (DH), a geometry-based visual sampler that compresses 2D images into compact 1D signals using paired golden-ratio-inspired spiral trajectories. Rather than processing every pixel uniformly, DH employs two phase-shifted helices (Alpha and Beta, offset by 180 degrees) to sample the image with biologically-inspired foveation: high density at the center, sparse coverage at the periphery. At 4K resolution, DH achieves a 1,433x compression ratio (99.93% reduction) while preserving the geometric structure of the scene. The full perception pipeline -- including spatial mapping, temporal collision detection, and intra-frame structural disparity estimation -- runs in 0.52 ms at 1080p on CPU-only hardware, with no neural network dependencies. On CIFAR-10 at extreme sampling budgets (K=128 points per helix), DH achieves a +6.03% accuracy gain over uniform random sampling. A JSON-serializable Robotics API is provided, delivering sub-millisecond spatial perception reports in 2.7 KB packets. Code and benchmarks are available under the MIT License.

AI.tldr: Double-Helix Vision (DH) is a geometry-based visual sampler that compresses images into 1D signals using spiral trajectories, achieving up to 1,433x compression ratio and running perception in 0.52ms on CPU-only hardware.
AI.motivation: To create an ultra-efficient, geometry-based visual sampling method that dramatically reduces data throughput while preserving spatial structure, without relying on neural networks or GPU acceleration.
AI.method: Uses two phase-shifted golden-ratio spiral trajectories (Alpha and Beta, 180° offset) to sample images with foveated density (high center, sparse periphery), then performs spatial mapping, temporal collision detection, and intra-frame disparity estimation on the compressed 1D signal.
AI.result: Achieves 1,433x compression at 4K (99.93% reduction), 0.52ms perception pipeline at 1080p on CPU, +6.03% accuracy gain over uniform random sampling on CIFAR-10 at K=128, and outputs 2.7KB JSON perception reports.
AI.conclusion: DH demonstrates that geometry-based spiral sampling can achieve extreme compression ratios and real-time performance on CPU-only hardware, offering a viable alternative to neural network-based perception for resource-constrained robotics applications.

title: JoyAI-VL-Interaction: Real-Time Vision-Language Interaction Intelligence
summary: Many moments in the real world do not wait for a user to ask. A fire starts on a security monitor, an expression flickers across a video call, or a product a viewer wants flashes by in a livestream. Yet today's large models remain mostly turn-based by design: they answer only when addressed, and even video-call apps that appear interactive still operate as question-answer systems, reacting only when polled or prompted. We argue for a different paradigm: a model that is present in the world like a person. It continuously watches what is happening now, decides on its own whether to speak or stay silent, interacts in real time, and delegates to a background model when the problem is hard. To advance interaction models and their adoption across domains, we make two fully open-sourced contributions. First, we release JoyAI-VL-Interaction, an 8B-scale, vision-first VL-interaction model. The model makes the response decision internally, choosing each second to stay silent, respond, or delegate to a background model, and it excels at vision-triggered responsiveness and time awareness. We pair it with a transferable training recipe, from which capabilities we never trained for emerge, such as guiding a shopper through changing app screens or improvising a lecture from a slide deck. Second, we release a complete, deployable system built around that model. The system streams any ongoing video into the model, making it genuinely present in the world. All other components are pluggable, including ASR/TTS modules, memory, visualization UI, and a background brain that can connect to any API or agent. Across six real-world scenarios, human raters prefer JoyAI-VL-Interaction over the in-app video-call assistants of Doubao and Gemini by a wide margin. To our knowledge, this is the first open, vision-driven interaction model released together with its training recipe, data, and complete deployable system.

AI.tldr: JoyAI-VL-Interaction is an open-source 8B vision-first interaction model that continuously watches the world, decides when to speak or stay silent, and can delegate hard problems to a background model. Paired with a deployable system, it outperforms Doubao and Gemini across six real-world scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current large models are turn-based (require user queries), but many real-world moments (fires, expressions, fleeting products) don't wait for a prompt. The authors argue for a paradigm where models are continuously present and autonomously decide when to interact.
AI.method: They build an 8B vision-first VL-interaction model that internally decides each second whether to stay silent, respond, or delegate to a background model. They also release a complete deployable system with pluggable ASR/TTS, memory, UI, and a background brain that connects to any API/agent.
AI.result: Human raters strongly prefer JoyAI-VL-Interaction over Doubao and Gemini's video-call assistants across six real-world scenarios. Emergent capabilities (e.g., guiding shoppers through app screens, improvising lectures from slides) appeared without explicit training.
AI.conclusion: This is the first open, vision-driven interaction model released with its training recipe, data, and complete deployable system, advancing a new paradigm of continuously present AI.

title: FactCheck: Feasibility-aware Long-term Action Anticipation with Multi-agent Collaboration
summary: Long-term action anticipation (LTA) aims to predict an ordered sequence of future verb-noun actions from a partially observed video. While this task serves as the foundation for embodied intelligence, anticipating physically feasible long-term actions remains a critical challenge. Existing methods, which operate in an open-loop manner, often hallucinate non-existent objects, violate object affordances, or disregard object states, as they lack explicit mechanisms to verify action feasibility against the physical environment. To address this, we propose FactCheck, a novel multi-agent collaboration framework that improves feasibility through a closed-loop "Observe-Plan-Verify" mechanism. FactCheck decomposes the complex LTA task into specialized roles: an Observer that recognizes historical actions from video observations and constructs a dual-form structured memory, comprising a History Action Abstract that captures high-level human intentions and environmental status, and a History Action Graph that encodes object states and temporal dependencies; a Planner that generates draft future actions conditioned on both low-level historical actions and high-level History Action Abstract; and a Verifier that rigorously validates the draft against the History Action Graph and refines infeasible actions. Extensive experiments on the EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+ benchmarks demonstrate that FactCheck consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Our work establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-aware long-term action anticipation, effectively closing the loop of action recognition, action prediction and action verification.

AI.tldr: FactCheck is a multi-agent framework for long-term action anticipation that uses a closed-loop "Observe-Plan-Verify" mechanism with Observer, Planner, and Verifier agents to ensure action feasibility, outperforming SOTA on EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+.
AI.motivation: Existing open-loop LTA methods lack explicit mechanisms to verify action feasibility, often hallucinating non-existent objects, violating object affordances, or disregarding object states.
AI.method: FactCheck decomposes LTA into three specialized roles: (1) Observer - recognizes historical actions and builds dual-form structured memory (History Action Abstract + History Action Graph); (2) Planner - generates draft future actions conditioned on historical actions and abstract; (3) Verifier - validates draft against History Action Graph and refines infeasible actions.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on EPIC-Kitchens-55 and EGTEA Gaze+ benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-aware long-term action anticipation, effectively closing the loop of action recognition, prediction, and verification.

title: YTClickbait21K: Human-Annotated Multimodal Dataset for YouTube Clickbait Detection Across Diverse Channels and Content Categories
summary: Clickbait content on video-sharing platforms poses a significant challenge to information reliability, yet progress in automated detection has been constrained by the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal datasets. We present YTClickbait21K, a human-annotated YouTube clickbait dataset comprising 21,238 videos collected from 40 channels across 29 countries, covering diverse content categories such as news, entertainment, education, and gaming. Each sample includes structured metadata (title, description, engagement statistics) along with associated thumbnail images, enabling comprehensive multimodal analysis. To ensure annotation quality, every video was independently labeled by three annotators using a standardized decision framework that incorporates textual, visual, and cross-modal consistency cues, with final labels determined through majority voting. The dataset exhibits substantial inter-annotator agreement (k=0.65), confirming reliable labeling despite the inherent subjectivity of clickbait detection. By combining scale, annotation rigor, and multimodal richness, this dataset provides a robust benchmark for developing and evaluating machine learning models, facilitating research in cross-modal semantic understanding, and advancing automated content moderation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents YTClickbait21K, a large-scale human-annotated multimodal YouTube clickbait dataset with 21,238 videos from 40 channels across 29 countries, featuring structured metadata and thumbnail images labeled by three annotators each with substantial agreement (k=0.65), serving as a benchmark for multimodal clickbait detection.
AI.motivation: Clickbait content on video-sharing platforms undermines information reliability, and progress in automated detection has been limited by the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal datasets.
AI.method: The authors constructed YTClickbait21K by collecting 21,238 videos from 40 YouTube channels across 29 countries covering diverse categories. Each sample includes structured metadata (title, description, engagement stats) and thumbnail images. Three independent annotators labeled each video using a standardized decision framework incorporating textual, visual, and cross-modal consistency cues, with final labels determined by majority voting.
AI.result: The dataset achieved substantial inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa = 0.65), confirming reliable labeling despite the inherent subjectivity of clickbait detection.
AI.conclusion: This dataset provides a robust benchmark for developing and evaluating machine learning models, facilitates research in cross-modal semantic understanding, and advances automated content moderation systems.

title: Variational Deep Unfolding with Mamba-Based Nonlocal Modeling for Underwater Image Enhancement
summary: Underwater imaging plays a crucial role in ocean engineering, although captured data often suffer from poor visibility and color distortion. To address these challenges, we propose a model-based deep unfolding network for underwater image enhancement that integrates variational modeling into a learnable architecture. The framework is guided by a variational formulation based on a dehazing decomposition, incorporating a multiplicative residual component to absorb remaining artifacts and a nonlocal gradient-type constraint to preserve structural details and enhance edge sharpness. We provide a theoretical analysis establishing the existence of solution for the associated minimization problem. The proposed unfolding method incorporates Mamba layers to efficiently capture self-similarities in the scene. In addition, we introduce a proximal trajectory loss that enforces consistency between the unfolding stages and the iterations of an ideal restoration regularizer. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed unfolding approach achieves improved visual quality and competitive quantitative performance compared with recent state-of-the-art methods. The source code will be available at https://github.com/MIA-UIB/Variational-Unfolding-Mamba-Underwater-Enhancement .

AI.tldr: A model-based deep unfolding network for underwater image enhancement that integrates variational modeling with Mamba layers and a novel proximal trajectory loss, achieving improved visual quality and competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Underwater imaging data suffer from poor visibility and color distortion, which limits their applicability in ocean engineering.
AI.method: Proposes a variational-model-based deep unfolding network with: (1) multiplicative residual for artifact absorption, (2) nonlocal gradient constraint for edge preservation, (3) Mamba layers for capturing self-similarities, and (4) proximal trajectory loss for stage-wise consistency.
AI.result: Achieves improved visual quality and competitive quantitative results compared to recent state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating variational modeling with deep unfolding and Mamba layers is an effective approach for underwater image enhancement, with theoretical soundness and practical performance gains.

title: Last But Not Least: Boundary Attention CalibratiON for Multimodal KV Cache Compression
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong vision-language reasoning, but long visual contexts enlarge the KV cache and increase decoding latency. Existing compression methods rely on observation window attention for stable token-importance estimation, yet this aggregation can dilute sparse visual evidence and discard answer-critical tokens under aggressive compression. Therefore, we identify last-query attention as a complementary source for recovering such evidence, but its answer-irrelevant signals can mislead retention. We propose BACON, a plug-and-play method that calibrates observation window attention with last-query evidence and suppresses isolated noise via intra-layer coherence and inter-layer persistence. Across diverse benchmarks, models, budgets, and compression methods, BACON improves multimodal KV compression by 7.5% on average under the most aggressive budget, with gains up to 30.9%.

AI.tldr: BACON is a plug-and-play method that improves multimodal KV cache compression by calibrating observation window attention with last-query attention, using intra-layer coherence and inter-layer persistence to suppress noise, achieving 7.5% average improvement under aggressive budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache compression methods for MLLMs rely on observation window attention, which can dilute sparse visual evidence and discard answer-critical tokens under aggressive compression.
AI.method: BACON calibrates observation window attention with complementary last-query attention evidence, and suppresses isolated noise via intra-layer coherence and inter-layer persistence mechanisms.
AI.result: BACON improves multimodal KV compression by 7.5% on average under the most aggressive budget, with gains up to 30.9% across diverse benchmarks, models, budgets, and compression methods.
AI.conclusion: BACON effectively addresses the limitations of existing compression methods by recovering answer-critical tokens lost in observation window attention, making it a robust plug-and-play solution for multimodal KV cache compression.

title: The Vision Encoder as a Privacy Boundary: Visual-Token Side Channels in Encoder-Free Vision-Language Models
summary: A vision encoder compresses image pixels into semantic embeddings, implicitly acting as a privacy boundary by preserving semantic content while attenuating pixel-local detail required for exact text recovery. Encoder-free vision-language models (VLMs) remove this boundary by routing image patches directly into the language-model token stream, thereby exposing an architectural privacy attack surface: intermediate visual tokens become a pre-output side channel. Under a token-access adversary, decoders invert visual-token streams from two encoder-free VLMs, Gemma4 and Fuyu, recovering recognizable image structure and readable held-out access codes, whereas matched encoder-based controls localize target regions but recover no exact strings. Within-model ablations show that the operative factor is spatial sampling fidelity of the visual-token grid, especially character-direction sampling density, rather than token or value count. The leakage is not limited to exported tokens: Gemma4 layer-0 key-value cache tensors are directly invertible, placing the side channel within KV caches commonly persisted by production serving stacks for decoding efficiency. The attack survives clutter, realistic document degradation, and zero-shot transfer to public document images, and it resists value-level defenses such as additive noise and quantization. Effective mitigation must therefore reduce spatial sampling, making removal of the vision encoder a first-class privacy decision in VLM deployment.

AI.tldr: Encoder-free VLMs route image patches directly into the language token stream, creating a privacy side channel where visual tokens can be inverted to recover readable text and images, unlike encoder-based models. The leakage stems from high spatial sampling fidelity, survives defenses like noise and quantization, and exists in KV caches, making vision encoder removal a key privacy consideration.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize a novel privacy attack surface introduced by encoder-free vision-language models, where the absence of a vision encoder exposes intermediate visual tokens as a side channel that can leak sensitive image content.
AI.method: Token-access adversaries perform decoding inversion on visual-token streams from two encoder-free VLMs (Gemma4 and Fuyu), compared against encoder-based controls. Within-model ablations isolate the operative factor (spatial sampling fidelity), and attacks are tested against KV cache tensors, document degradation, zero-shot transfer, and value-level defenses.
AI.result: Encoder-free VLMs leak recognizable image structure and readable access codes; encoder-based models localize regions but recover no exact strings. The key factor is spatial sampling fidelity (character-direction density), not token count. Layer-0 KV caches are also directly invertible. Attacks survive clutter, degradation, zero-shot transfer, and resist additive noise/quantization.
AI.conclusion: Removing the vision encoder is a first-class privacy decision in VLM deployment. Effective mitigation must reduce spatial sampling fidelity rather than rely on value-level defenses.

title: Vision-Encoder Behavioral Fingerprints of Image-to-Image Generative Models: A Training-Paradigm-Driven Taxonomy of Six Commercial APIs
summary: We study six production image-to-image AI systems (gpt-image-1, Gemini 2.5 Flash Image, Flux Kontext, SDXL img2img, SD3 img2img, and Qwen Image Edit) under a content-adaptive sub-JND adversarial perturbation pipeline, scoring all outputs by frozen DINOv2 ViT-B/14 token distances against clean references. Across a 3,588-call corpus spanning COCO photographs, CelebA-HQ portraits, and AI-generated inputs, the six systems partition into two image-invariant behavioral bands on a 2D (patch_mean, ssim_clean) plane: edit-trained models (Flux Kontext, Qwen Edit, Gemini) cluster in a tight band, while T2I-base models adapted at sampling time (SDXL, SD3, gpt-image-1) cluster in a drift band.

AI.tldr: Six image-to-image AI systems were tested using adversarial perturbations and showed two distinct behavioral clusters: edit-trained models (Flux Kontext, Qwen Edit, Gemini) and T2I-base models adapted at sampling time (SDXL, SD3, gpt-image-1).
AI.motivation: To systematically compare and characterize behavioral differences across production image-to-image AI systems under adversarial conditions.
AI.method: Applied content-adaptive sub-JND adversarial perturbation pipeline; scored outputs using frozen DINOv2 ViT-B/14 token distances; analyzed behavior on a 2D (patch_mean, ssim_clean) plane across 3,588 calls covering COCO, CelebA-HQ, and AI-generated inputs.
AI.result: Six systems partition into two image-invariant behavioral bands: edit-trained models cluster in a tight band, T2I-base models adapted at sampling time cluster in a drift band.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental behavioral dichotomy between edit-trained and T2I-base image-to-image models under adversarial perturbations.

title: Efficient Reinforcement for Visual-Textual Thinking with Discrete Diffusion Model
summary: RL-based post-training has been widely adopted to enable interleaved visual and textual reasoning in unified multimodal models capable of both text and image generation. However, most existing approaches are built upon autoregressive (AR) unified models, which require full image regeneration during visual reasoning. In this work, we demonstrate that multimodal discrete diffusion models are effective alternatives to AR models for reinforcement learning in interleaved reasoning, owing to their ability to perform efficient visual rollouts via localized visual editing rather than full image-token regeneration. This reduces rollout computation during GRPO by 26.9\% compared to AR baselines, with minimal performance drop. Despite the improved efficiency, we find that joint reward assignment, which employs a shared reward signal across modalities, introduces cross-modal interference between unrelated image and text token sequences during RL updates. To address this issue, we propose factorized reward assignment, a strategy that assigns rewards independently to text and vision segments. With factorized reward assignment, our RL approach achieves an 11.2% improvement over joint reward assignment and a 38.04% improvement over the base model.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that multimodal discrete diffusion models can replace autoregressive models for RL-based post-training in interleaved visual-textual reasoning, achieving 26.9% computation savings via localized editing. It also proposes factorized reward assignment to address cross-modal interference, yielding 11.2% improvement over joint reward assignment and 38.04% over the base model.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based post-training for interleaved visual-textual reasoning relies on autoregressive unified models, which require full image regeneration during visual reasoning—this is computationally expensive. The authors aim to find a more efficient alternative.
AI.method: (1) Use multimodal discrete diffusion models instead of AR models for efficient visual rollouts via localized editing. (2) Propose factorized reward assignment that assigns rewards independently to text and vision segments to avoid cross-modal interference.
AI.result: (1) Reduces rollout computation during GRPO by 26.9% compared to AR baselines with minimal performance drop. (2) Factorized reward assignment achieves 11.2% improvement over joint reward assignment and 38.04% improvement over the base model.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal discrete diffusion models are effective and efficient alternatives to AR models for RL-based post-training in interleaved reasoning, and factorized reward assignment mitigates cross-modal interference, significantly boosting performance.

title: Position: The Systemic Lack of Agency in Visual Reasoning
summary: This paper argues that a systemic lack of Agency constrains the implicit reasoning capabilities of current Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Implicit reasoning refers to the ability to autonomously discover and utilize hidden visual evidence to bridge information gaps, rather than merely relying on explicitly specified targets. This capacity underlies human visual understanding and everyday reasoning. We argue that this limitation arises from a tendency to approach visual reasoning primarily as passive semantic retrieval, rather than as active, situated reasoning that depends on autonomous visual exploration. As a result, most existing benchmarks primarily assess Passive Capacity, leaving this aspect of reasoning largely unmeasured. To address this gap, we introduce the Visual Implicit Reasoning Diagnosing Benchmark (V-IRD), which targets this missing quadrant by requiring models to derive answers strictly through autonomous visual analysis. Our results show that, despite strong retrieval abilities, prominent VLMs struggle to utilize reference objects and to attend to visual evidence that requires self-directed inquiry. Simply put, strong semantic recognition does not equate to active visual exploration, revealing a critical gap in current VLMs. More information can be found at https://haoychen.github.io/Implicit-Reasoning/

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that VLMs lack Agency for implicit reasoning (autonomous visual exploration), introduces the V-IRD benchmark to measure this gap, and shows that strong semantic recognition ≠ active visual exploration.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs treat visual reasoning as passive semantic retrieval rather than active, situated reasoning requiring autonomous visual exploration, leaving implicit reasoning capabilities largely unmeasured.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Visual Implicit Reasoning Diagnosing Benchmark (V-IRD), which requires models to derive answers strictly through autonomous visual analysis rather than relying on explicitly specified targets.
AI.result: Prominent VLMs struggle to utilize reference objects and attend to visual evidence requiring self-directed inquiry, despite having strong retrieval abilities. Strong semantic recognition does not equate to active visual exploration.
AI.conclusion: A systemic lack of Agency constrains implicit reasoning in VLMs, revealing a critical gap that current benchmarks fail to measure.

title: HSQ-VLM: A Novel Spatially-Constrained Quadrant Segmentation VLM Model for Explainability in Diabetic Retinopathy
summary: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is an aggressive retinal disease and a leading cause of global blindness, yet its clinical management is currently hindered by the black-box nature of diagnostic AI. While deep learning models achieve high classification accuracy, there is a critical lack of explainability methods capable of detailing the exact anatomical landmarks and lesion distributions that lead to a clinical decision for DR. Therefore, we propose HSQ-VLM, a novel quadrant segmentation pipeline on fundus images that utilizes a Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention mechanism to unify visual feature extraction with structured clinical reasoning. Unlike traditional methods that rely on arbitrary image partitioning, our pipeline implements 4-quadrant Topological Latent Partitioning (TLP) to dynamically align retinal features with a fovea-centered coordinate system. This allows the Vision-Language Model to generate natural language reports that quantify pathology with anatomical precision. On a dataset of 3,500 high-resolution fundus images, this innovative methodology achieved a lesion detection sensitivity of 99.6% for hemorrhages and 96.4% for microaneurysms, while demonstrating a significant reduction in boundary-ambiguity errors compared to standard segmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: HSQ-VLM proposes a quadrant segmentation pipeline for DR diagnosis that uses Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention and Topological Latent Partitioning to generate anatomically precise, explainable reports, achieving 99.6% hemorrhage and 96.4% microaneurysm sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Current DR diagnostic AI models are black-box systems lacking explainability about anatomical landmarks and lesion distributions that lead to clinical decisions.
AI.method: HSQ-VLM uses a quadrant segmentation pipeline with Landmark-Anchored Cartesian Cross-Attention and 4-quadrant Topological Latent Partitioning (TLP) aligned to a fovea-centered coordinate system, enabling Vision-Language Models to generate precise natural language reports.
AI.result: On 3,500 fundus images: 99.6% hemorrhage detection sensitivity, 96.4% microaneurysm detection sensitivity, and significant reduction in boundary-ambiguity errors over baselines.
AI.conclusion: HSQ-VLM successfully unifies visual feature extraction with structured clinical reasoning, providing explainable DR diagnosis with high accuracy and anatomical precision.

title: S23DR 2026: End-to-End 3D Wireframe Prediction via DETR-Style Set Prediction with Contrastive Denoising
summary: We present WireframeDETR, our submission to the Structured Semantic 3D Reconstruction (S23DR) 2026 Challenge, which requires predicting a 3D building wireframe from multi-view COLMAP point clouds. Our method applies DETR-style set prediction directly to 3D point clouds, producing wireframes as sets of edge coordinate pairs without any intermediate vertex detection stage. We introduce three technical contributions: (1) contrastive denoising training that stabilises noisy Hungarian matching in early epochs; (2) a multi-scale encoder that aggregates the last encoder layer outputs via learned scalar weights; and (3) progressive auxiliary loss weighting that concentrates gradient signal on the decoder layers that most benefit from it. Our model achieves a public test HSS of 0.575 (F1~=~0.664, IoU~=~0.516) and a best validation HSS of 0.534 on the cleaned val split.

AI.tldr: WireframeDETR is a DETR-style model that directly predicts 3D building wireframes from multi-view COLMAP point clouds without intermediate vertex detection, achieving 0.575 HSS on the S23DR 2026 Challenge test set.
AI.motivation: To address the Structured Semantic 3D Reconstruction (S23DR) 2026 Challenge by predicting 3D building wireframes from multi-view COLMAP point clouds.
AI.method: Applies DETR-style set prediction directly to 3D point clouds, producing wireframes as sets of edge coordinate pairs without intermediate vertex detection. Three contributions: (1) contrastive denoising training, (2) multi-scale encoder with learned scalar weights, (3) progressive auxiliary loss weighting.
AI.result: Public test HSS of 0.575 (F1=0.664, IoU=0.516), best validation HSS of 0.534 on cleaned val split.
AI.conclusion: WireframeDETR successfully demonstrates that direct set prediction for 3D wireframes from point clouds is viable, with three technical contributions improving training stability and performance.

title: Multi-HMR 2: Multi-Person Camera-Centric Human Detection, Mesh Recovery and Tracking
summary: Most advances in human mesh recovery (HMR) have focused on pelvis-centered recovery, overlooking metric 3D localization and detection accuracy in the camera coordinate system - two key factors for real-world applications such as human-robot interaction and social scene understanding. Current evaluation protocols often ignore these aspects, emphasizing per-person, root-centered recovery rather than camera-space perception. As a result, existing approaches rely on fixed camera assumptions or handcrafted post-processing, limiting their robustness and practical deployment. We introduce Multi-HMR 2, a simple yet robust DETR-based framework for Multi-person Camera-centric Human detection, mesh Recovery, and tracking. Multi-HMR 2 predicts a scene-consistent camera together with human meshes, enabling metric 3D localization without ground-truth intrinsics. Moreover, by distilling image-based memory features from SAM2, Multi-HMR 2 extends to tracking, achieving consistent identity association without video supervision. Despite its conceptual simplicity - no handcrafted components, no video input, and no ground-truth cameras - Multi-HMR 2 achieves state-of-the-art pelvis-centered performance while substantially improving detection accuracy and metric 3D localization.

AI.tldr: Multi-HMR 2 is a DETR-based framework that jointly predicts scene-consistent cameras and human meshes, achieving metric 3D localization and tracking without relying on fixed camera assumptions, handcrafted post-processing, or video supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing HMR methods focus on pelvis-centered recovery but neglect metric 3D localization and detection accuracy in camera coordinates, which are critical for real-world applications like human-robot interaction and social scene understanding.
AI.method: A DETR-based framework that jointly predicts a scene-consistent camera with human meshes, and distills image-based memory features from SAM2 to enable tracking without video supervision.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art pelvis-centered performance while substantially improving detection accuracy and metric 3D localization.
AI.conclusion: Despite its simplicity (no handcrafted components, no video input, no ground-truth cameras), Multi-HMR 2 robustly addresses camera-space perception and tracking for real-world deployment.

title: An Ensemble Deep Learning Approach for Reliable and Scalable Lemon Leaf Disease Classification
comment: 5 pages, 12 figures, 3 Tables, Presented at 18th IEEE International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Communication Networks (CICN) 2026
summary: Early detection of plant diseases is crucial to plants and for the farmers. Plant diseases reduce fruit yield and quality, and plants are more susceptible to other stresses when they are infected. The lemon leaf disease dataset contains 1354 images. The dataset has 9 classes. Among the 9 classes only one class is for healthy leaf, and the other 8 classes are leaf diseases. The dataset was split into training (70%), testing (15%) and validation (15%) sets after comprehensive preprocessing. Two pretrained models (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) were applied and then combined these models using an ensemble technique to boost robustness. Ensemble models showed a promising performance of 99.27% accuracy. Adversarial Training is applied to improve models' ability and ensure reliable predictions under noisy data. Grad-CAM visualization highlights the important regions of leaf images that validate the model prediction with confidence level.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an ensemble of InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 with adversarial training for lemon leaf disease detection, achieving 99.27% accuracy on a 9-class (8 diseases + 1 healthy) dataset of 1354 images.
AI.motivation: Early detection of plant diseases is critical to prevent yield loss, fruit quality reduction, and increased susceptibility to other stresses.
AI.method: Two pretrained CNNs (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) were ensembled, combined with adversarial training for robustness, and evaluated using Grad-CAM for interpretability on a 1354-image lemon leaf dataset split 70/15/15.
AI.result: The ensemble model achieved 99.27% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The ensemble with adversarial training provides reliable and interpretable plant disease classification, robust to noisy data.

title: Understanding Cross-Modal Contributions in Continual Vision-Language Models: A Theoretical Perspective
summary: Continual vision-language models are commonly addressed through sequential fine-tuning; however, although this paradigm enables adaptation to new environments (tasks), it inherently emphasizes the contribution of previously learned environments (tasks) at the expense of the stability required to preserve previously acquired knowledge. While existing approaches have adequately studied continual learning and catastrophic forgetting in vision-language models (VLMs), the theoretical understanding of modality-specific contributions across a sequence of environments remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a new theoretical perspective to understand the cross-modal (vision-language) contributions to consecutive environments. We empirically evaluate our theoretical findings on large VLMs and demonstrate their effectiveness in capturing environment-level cross-modal contributions. Our analysis provides deeper insights into continual VLMs, highlighting their contribution robustness to varying task orders and inter-task similarities, and their improved generalization performance.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding cross-modal (vision-language) contributions in continual learning settings, demonstrating that modality-specific analysis helps explain and improve robustness to task order and inter-task similarities in vision-language models.
AI.motivation: While continual learning in VLMs has been studied, the theoretical understanding of how vision and language modalities individually contribute across sequential tasks remains unexplored, motivating the need for a cross-modal theoretical perspective.
AI.method: The authors present a new theoretical perspective to analyze cross-modal contributions in continual VLMs, empirically evaluating their framework on large vision-language models to capture environment-level modality-specific contributions.
AI.result: The theoretical findings empirically demonstrate effectiveness in capturing cross-modal contributions, showing robustness to varying task orders and inter-task similarities, along with improved generalization performance.
AI.conclusion: Understanding modality-specific contributions provides deeper insights into continual VLMs, highlighting that cross-modal analysis can improve robustness and generalization in sequential learning settings.

title: Improved Knowledge Distillation for Land-Use Image Classification
summary: In the present article, an improved Knowledge Distillation (KD) framework has been proposed for efficient compression of deep convolutional neural networks for land-use image classification task. Motivated by the need to achieve competitive classification accuracy while reducing computational complexity, a teacher-student learning paradigm is adopted in which a VGG16 network transfers knowledge to a lightweight MobileNetV2 model. The proposed framework integrates hard supervision from ground truth labels with a soft supervision strategy that combines Kullback-Leibler divergence and Cosine Similarity losses. Experiments conducted on three land-use datasets show that the proposed KD-based method yields improved performance, and achieves an accuracy of 99.04%, outperforming both baseline student training and single-loss distillation approaches, while retaining substantial model compression.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an improved Knowledge Distillation framework using VGG16 as teacher and MobileNetV2 as student, combining KL divergence and Cosine Similarity losses for land-use image classification, achieving 99.04% accuracy with efficient model compression.
AI.motivation: To achieve competitive land-use classification accuracy while reducing computational complexity through efficient model compression.
AI.method: Teacher-student learning (VGG16 → MobileNetV2) integrating hard supervision (ground truth labels) with soft supervision (KL divergence + Cosine Similarity losses).
AI.result: Achieved 99.04% accuracy on three land-use datasets, outperforming baseline student training and single-loss distillation approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed KD framework effectively balances high accuracy and model compression for land-use classification, surpassing both standard training and simpler distillation methods.

title: Deep Learning in Seismic Interpretation: Federated Advances in Salt Dome Segmentation
summary: Salt-dome delineation is a critical, high-impact task in subsurface geological interpretation, driving decisions in hydrocarbon exploration, reservoir modeling, and drilling safety. While convolutional encoder-decoder architectures have delivered significant improvements in automated salt segmentation, their widespread application is severely limited by data sovereignty concerns, dataset bias, and the scarcity of labeled seismic volumes. This paper introduces FedSaltNet, a Federated Learning (FL) framework explicitly engineered for robust, generalizable, and privacy preserving salt-dome segmentation. We couple a lightweight Small U-Net backbone, chosen for its efficiency and regularization properties with a novel Foreground-Weighted (FG-WEIGHTED) aggregation strategy designed to tackle domain-specific class imbalance. Through an extensive comparative study emulating non-IID conditions across four diverse seismic datasets (TGS, SEAM, F3, GBS), we demonstrate two critical findings: The FG-WEIGHTED algorithm effectively mitigates data heterogeneity, yielding a 4.0% relative improvement in Intersection over Union (IoU) over the best conventional FL method. The simple U-Net architecture proved essential, outperforming the higher capacity ResNet-18 U-Net variant by 166% in average IoU, underscoring the necessity of architectural simplicity in data-constrained federated environments. FedSaltNet provides a validated, high-performance solution that establishes the viability of federated deep learning for collaborative, next-generation subsurface interpretation.

AI.tldr: FedSaltNet introduces a Federated Learning framework with a lightweight Small U-Net and Foreground-Weighted aggregation strategy for privacy-preserving salt-dome segmentation, achieving 4% IoU improvement over conventional FL methods across four seismic datasets.
AI.motivation: Salt-dome delineation is critical for hydrocarbon exploration and drilling safety, but existing automated segmentation methods face severe limitations from data sovereignty concerns, dataset bias, and scarcity of labeled seismic volumes.
AI.method: FedSaltNet combines a lightweight Small U-Net backbone (chosen for efficiency and regularization) with a novel Foreground-Weighted (FG-WEIGHTED) aggregation strategy to address domain-specific class imbalance in non-IID federated settings.
AI.result: FG-WEIGHTED achieved 4.0% relative IoU improvement over the best conventional FL method, and the simple U-Net outperformed ResNet-18 U-Net by 166% in average IoU across four diverse seismic datasets (TGS, SEAM, F3, GBS).
AI.conclusion: FedSaltNet establishes the viability of federated deep learning for collaborative, next-generation subsurface interpretation, demonstrating that architectural simplicity is crucial in data-constrained federated environments.

title: Mask Proposal Voting Based on Geodesic Framework for Robust Image Segmentation
summary: Despite great advances, finding accurate segmentation remains a challenging task, especially in scenarios with cluttered backgrounds, complex intensity variations and topology appearance. Minimal path models have exhibited their strong ability in addressing image segmentation tasks. However, the performance of minimal paths-based segmentation approaches is heavily influenced by model initialization, hence limiting their application scope in practice. In this work, we propose a novel mask proposal voting framework that overcomes the major drawback of classical approaches, allowing robust segmentation even in complicated scenarios. Firstly, we introduce an efficient method for constructing adaptive domain cuts as a constraint for initializing the region-based min-cut evolution, by which diverse and reliable mask proposal candidates can be generated, substantially increasing the possibility of accurately covering the objective region by these proposals. Secondly, we propose a new mask voting scheme to build a voting score map encoding the final segmentation information. In contrast to classical path voting methods, our model allows incorporating priors to assign different importance to each individual mask. As a consequence, the proposed segmentation model is capable of accurately delineating object boundaries under complex scenarios, and is insensitive to initialization. Experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art minimal path-based approaches in both accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mask proposal voting framework for minimal path-based segmentation that overcomes initialization sensitivity by generating adaptive domain cuts for diverse mask proposals and using a weighted voting scheme with priors, achieving robust and accurate segmentation in complex scenarios.
AI.motivation: Minimal path-based segmentation methods are highly sensitive to model initialization, which limits their practical applicability in cluttered backgrounds, complex intensity variations, and challenging topology appearance scenarios.
AI.method: 1) An efficient method for constructing adaptive domain cuts that constrain region-based min-cut evolution to generate diverse and reliable mask proposal candidates. 2) A novel mask voting scheme that builds a voting score map incorporating priors to assign different importance to each individual mask, unlike classical path voting methods.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art minimal path-based approaches in both accuracy and robustness across complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The mask proposal voting framework effectively eliminates initialization sensitivity of minimal path models, enabling accurate object boundary delineation under complex conditions.

title: FlexPooling with Simple Auxiliary Classifiers in Deep Networks
summary: In computer vision, the basic pipeline of most convolutional neural networks consists of multiple feature extraction layers, where the input signal is downsampled to a lower resolution in each subsequent layer. This downsampling process is commonly referred to as pooling, which is an essential operation in CNNs. Pooling improves robustness against transformations, reduces the number of trainable parameters, increases the receptive field, and lowers computation time. Since pooling is a lossy process but remains important for extracting high-level information from low-level representations, it is important to preserve the most prominent information from previous activations to improve network discriminability. Standard pooling is usually performed using dense pooling methods, such as max pooling or average pooling, or through strided convolutional kernels. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective adaptive pooling method, called FlexPooling, which generalizes average pooling by learning a weighted average over activations jointly with the rest of the network. We further show that attaching Simple Auxiliary Classifiers (SAC) to the CNN improves performance and demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with standard pooling methods. Experiments on multiple popular image classification datasets show that FlexPooling consistently outperforms baseline networks, achieving approximately 1 to 3 percent improvement in accuracy.

AI.tldr: FlexPooling is an adaptive pooling method that learns weighted averages over activations, outperforming standard pooling by 1-3% accuracy on image classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Pooling is essential in CNNs but is lossy; standard dense pooling methods (max, average, strided conv) may not preserve the most prominent information needed for discriminability.
AI.method: Proposes FlexPooling, which generalizes average pooling by learning a weighted average over activations jointly with network training. Also uses Simple Auxiliary Classifiers (SAC) to boost performance.
AI.result: FlexPooling consistently improves accuracy by 1-3% over baseline networks on multiple popular image classification datasets.
AI.conclusion: FlexPooling is a simple yet effective adaptive pooling method that outperforms standard pooling approaches, especially when combined with SAC.

title: Learning Sparse Latent Predictive Foundation Model for Multimodal Neuroimaging
summary: Brain MRIs are routinely acquired as multiple complementary sequences with unique contrast weighting, including T1-weighed imaging (T1w) anatomic and fluid-sensitive T2-weighted (T2w) contrasts. However, methods for learning unified representations across the multitude of MRI contrast mechanisms at health-system scale are lacking. In this study, we introduce Neuro-JEPA, a sparse multimodal neuroimaging foundation model that combines a latent predictive objective with a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to encode brain MRI across core T1w, T2w, and fluid-suppressed FLAIR imaging (FLAIR). We further provide a systematic methodological study of architectural, masking, objective, and sparsity design choices beneficial for robust neuroimaging multimodal representation learning. Neuro-JEPA was pretrained on 1,551,862 scans from 428,647 studies after modality-specific preprocessing with data curation across three core structural brain MRI sequences. We evaluated the learned representations across clinical and research settings, including 25 tasks from three health systems: NYU Langone, NYU Long Island, and Massachusetts General Hospital, and 22 tasks from 12 public datasets, covering unimodal, multimodal and cross-domain evaluation configurations. Across these benchmarks, existing neuroimaging foundation models showed inconsistent gains over a simple convolutional neural network (CNN) baseline, whereas Neuro-JEPA achieved stronger and more consistent performance across all evaluated settings. These results establish a scalable methodological framework for multimodal neuroimaging representation learning and highlight the need for foundation model evaluation protocols that include simple baselines, clinically heterogeneous cohorts and controlled multimodal comparisons.

AI.tldr: Neuro-JEPA is a multimodal neuroimaging foundation model pretrained on over 1.5 million scans across T1w, T2w, and FLAIR sequences, using a latent predictive objective and Mixture-of-Experts architecture. It outperforms existing foundation models across 47 tasks from 3 health systems and 12 public datasets, while highlighting that prior models showed inconsistent gains over simple CNN baselines.
AI.motivation: Current methods lack the ability to learn unified representations across multiple MRI contrast mechanisms (T1w, T2w, FLAIR) at health-system scale. Existing neuroimaging foundation models also show inconsistent improvements over simple baselines, motivating a more robust multimodal framework.
AI.method: Neuro-JEPA combines a latent predictive objective with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to encode core structural brain MRI sequences (T1w, T2w, FLAIR). The authors systematically study architectural, masking, objective, and sparsity design choices. The model is pretrained on 1,551,862 scans from 428,647 studies across three sequences.
AI.result: Evaluated on 25 tasks from three health systems and 22 tasks from 12 public datasets (unimodal, multimodal, cross-domain). Existing neuroimaging foundation models showed inconsistent gains over a simple CNN baseline, whereas Neuro-JEPA achieved stronger and more consistent performance across all settings.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-JEPA establishes a scalable methodological framework for multimodal neuroimaging representation learning. The results emphasize the need for evaluation protocols that include simple baselines, clinically heterogeneous cohorts, and controlled multimodal comparisons when assessing foundation models.

title: MVEB: Massive Video Embedding Benchmark
summary: We introduce the Massive Video Embedding Benchmark (MVEB), a 23-task benchmark for video embeddings spanning classification, zero-shot classification, clustering, pair classification, retrieval, and video-centric question answering. We evaluate 33 models and find that no single model dominates: MLLM-based embeddings lead on classification, clustering, pair classification, and QA; multimodal binding leads on retrieval and zero-shot classification; generative MLLMs without contrastive adaptation collapse on cross-modal tasks. Paired video-only vs. audio+video evaluations show that audio's contribution depends on dataset annotation provenance: audio helps when labels were produced from both modalities and hurts when they were produced from visuals alone, a six-point gap consistent across model families. MVEB is derived from MVEB+, a 184-task pool, and is designed to maintain task diversity while reducing evaluation cost. It integrates into the MTEB ecosystem for unified evaluation across text, image, audio, and video. We release MVEB and all 184 tasks along with code and a leaderboard at https://github.com/embeddings-benchmark/mteb.

AI.tldr: MVEB is a 23-task benchmark for evaluating video embeddings across 33 models, revealing that no single model type dominates all tasks, and that audio's contribution depends on annotation modality.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive, standardized benchmark for video embeddings that covers diverse tasks and enables systematic comparison of models, while reducing evaluation cost.
AI.method: Constructed MVEB (23 tasks) from MVEB+ (184 tasks) pool, evaluating 33 models on classification, zero-shot classification, clustering, pair classification, retrieval, and video QA tasks. Analyzed audio contribution via paired video-only vs. audio+video evaluations.
AI.result: No single model dominates; MLLM-based embeddings excel at classification, clustering, pair classification, and QA; multimodal binding leads on retrieval and zero-shot classification. Audio helps when labels come from both modalities but hurts when from visuals alone (6-point gap).
AI.conclusion: MVEB provides a unified video embedding benchmark integrated into MTEB ecosystem, revealing task-specific model strengths and modality annotation biases.

title: Multi-Modal Attention for Automated Disaster Damage Assessment Using Remote Sensing Imagery and Deep Learning
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in ISPRS Congress 2026 and the 47th Canadian Symposium on Remote Sensing (CSRS 2026) Annals
summary: Timely and accurate disaster damage assessment is crucial for effective emergency response, resource allocation, and recovery. Traditional methods, which often rely on manual inspections or sparse data, are typically slow and error-prone. This paper introduces a novel framework leveraging remote sensing imagery and deep learning to automate building damage classification. Using pre- and post-disaster satellite imagery, our model categorizes buildings into four damage levels: no damage, minor damage, major damage, and destroyed. The core innovation is a multi-modal attention mechanism that fuses bi-temporal features to explicitly detect and assess structural changes. We employ a lightweight ConvNeXT-Tiny backbone to ensure efficient processing without compromising performance. Key contributions include: (1) a cross-attention module for multi-modal data fusion, (2) an optimized preprocessing pipeline for large-scale datasets, and (3) robust data augmentation techniques. Experiments on a large-scale disaster dataset demonstrate an overall classification accuracy of 94.90%. The model effectively discriminates between damage categories and remains resilient to incomplete data. This system significantly improves assessment speed and accuracy, aiding emergency responders in prioritizing interventions. This work advances automated disaster damage detection by integrating multi-temporal imagery with deep learning, offering a scalable solution for real-time response.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework using multi-modal attention on pre/post-disaster satellite imagery achieves 94.90% accuracy in classifying building damage into four levels, enabling faster and more reliable disaster assessment.
AI.motivation: Traditional disaster damage assessment relies on manual inspections or sparse data, which are slow and error-prone. There is a pressing need for automated, accurate, and timely methods to support emergency response and resource allocation.
AI.method: The framework uses pre- and post-disaster satellite imagery with a multi-modal attention mechanism (cross-attention module) to fuse bi-temporal features and detect structural changes. A lightweight ConvNeXT-Tiny backbone ensures efficiency, with optimized preprocessing and robust data augmentation techniques.
AI.result: Experiments on a large-scale disaster dataset achieve 94.90% overall classification accuracy. The model effectively discriminates among four damage categories (no damage, minor, major, destroyed) and remains resilient to incomplete data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system significantly improves assessment speed and accuracy over traditional methods, offering a scalable solution for real-time disaster response and advancing automated damage detection through multi-temporal deep learning.

title: ReGenHuman: Re-Generating Human Appearances for Realistic Full-Body Video Anonymization
summary: Anonymizing human-centric video data is an understudied problem. Prior anonymization techniques either blur or redact pixels at the cost of realism and downstream utility, or generate frame-by-frame at the cost of temporal coherence. We introduce ReGenHuman, the first full-body video anonymization pipeline that is simultaneously realistic, temporally consistent, and anonymous by construction. Contrary to past approaches which redact or edit the inputs directly, we propose a regenerate, don't edit paradigm. Our approach composites 2D pose, segmentation, and monocular depth into two complementary conditioning streams - StructAll and StructHuman, which are used to fine-tune a video-to-video diffusion backbone on in-the-wild human videos, synthesizing the human regions entirely from identity-free structural cues. We evaluate our model on privacy, quality, and utility, and show that our ReGenHuman achieves the best tradeoff across all three axes against current baselines. We further show that our anonymized videos remain effective for downstream tasks, including video question answering.

AI.tldr: ReGenHuman is a full-body video anonymization pipeline that regenerates human regions from identity-free structural cues using video diffusion, achieving realistic, temporally consistent, and anonymous results superior to blurring/redaction baselines.
AI.motivation: Human-centric video anonymization is understudied; existing methods sacrifice realism, temporal coherence, or downstream utility by blurring, redacting, or frame-by-frame generation.
AI.method: Proposes a "regenerate, don't edit" paradigm: composites 2D pose, segmentation, and monocular depth into two conditioning streams (StructAll and StructHuman) to fine-tune a video-to-video diffusion backbone, synthesizing human regions from identity-free structural cues.
AI.result: ReGenHuman achieves the best tradeoff across privacy, quality, and utility compared to baselines; anonymized videos remain effective for downstream tasks like video question answering.
AI.conclusion: Regenerating human regions from structural cues instead of editing inputs yields realistic, temporally coherent, and anonymous videos that preserve utility.

title: NEXUS: Neural Energy Fields for Physically Consistent Contact-Rich 3D Object Dynamics
summary: Physics-grounded video generation requires controllable 3D object dynamics that remain physically consistent under contact, deformation, and external forcing. Existing trajectory-based methods often model isolated physical effects, making it difficult to compose conservative and non-conservative dynamics in contact-rich 3D scenes. We present NEXUS, a neural energy-field framework for contact-rich 3D object dynamics. NEXUS represents each object as a structural graph and constructs dynamic object-object and object-environment contact graphs. Inspired by Hamiltonian Neural Networks, NEXUS formulates motion through scalar energy and dissipation terms rather than directly predicting states or accelerations. Conservative effects, including gravity and elastic deformation, are composed as additive energy terms, while non-conservative effects such as damping and impact-induced energy loss are modeled with learned Rayleigh-style dissipation. Forces are derived by differentiating the energy and dissipation functions and rolled out with a multi-substep semi-implicit integrator. Across controlled trajectory benchmarks, NEXUS improves long-horizon accuracy over representative learned and physics-structured dynamics baselines under varying mechanical properties and physical-effect compositions. We further show that NEXUS trajectories provide effective guidance for contact-rich video generation, improving physical plausibility while maintaining competitive visual quality.

AI.tldr: NEXUS is a neural energy-field framework for contact-rich 3D object dynamics that models both conservative and non-conservative forces through scalar energy and dissipation terms, achieving improved long-horizon accuracy and enabling physically plausible video generation.
AI.motivation: Existing trajectory-based methods for physics-grounded video generation model isolated physical effects, making it difficult to compose conservative and non-conservative dynamics in contact-rich 3D scenes.
AI.method: NEXUS represents objects as structural graphs with dynamic contact graphs, formulates motion via Hamiltonian-inspired scalar energy and dissipation terms (gravity, elastic deformation as additive energy; damping, impact loss as Rayleigh dissipation), and rolls out dynamics with a multi-substep semi-implicit integrator.
AI.result: NEXUS improves long-horizon trajectory accuracy over learned and physics-structured baselines under varying mechanical properties. NEXUS trajectories improve physical plausibility in contact-rich video generation while maintaining competitive visual quality.
AI.conclusion: Modeling object dynamics through composable energy and dissipation terms is effective for accurate long-horizon simulation of contact-rich 3D scenes and benefits downstream video generation tasks.

title: Towards Global AI-Driven Cervical Cancer Screening
summary: The global elimination of cervical cancer is a key public health goal set by the World Health Organization (WHO), with screening programs reducing mortality by up to 80%. However, access to experts and biopsy services is limited in low- to middle-income countries (LMICs). Deep learning (DL)-based algorithms offer promising support for screening, but most existing approaches have been developed and validated on private datasets from single countries. We present the first DL-based approach to cervical cancer screening validated on data from multiple countries. Technically, we phrase the problem of detecting and classifying lesions in colposcopy images as a multi-task learning problem, in which we simultaneously perform image-level classification and lesion segmentation. Our model was trained on a private data set of acid stain colposcopy images with manually generated lesion segmentation masks and corresponding histopathological results, employing extensive data augmentation to address image variability. In an in-distribution validation with pathology results serving as ground truth, our algorithm outperformed medical experts (Balanced Accuracy: 0.68 vs 0.64) in CIN1- (Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 1 or lower) versus CIN2+ (grade 2 or higher) classification. External validation on four colposcopy data sets from four countries featuring radical differences in prevalence and patient characteristics yielded superior performance of our method compared to baseline methods. Performance variability across countries was high with AUC values ranging from 0.54 - 0.80. Overall, algorithm performance varied with age, transformation zone (cervical area most prone to lesion development), presence of comorbidities and pathognomonic signs, with comorbidities having by far the largest negative effect. Future work should focus on improving model robustness and generalizability.

AI.tldr: A deep learning model for cervical cancer screening trained on colposcopy images, outperforming experts on in-distribution data but showing high performance variability across four countries (AUC 0.54-0.80), with comorbidities negatively impacting accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the global need for cervical cancer screening access in low- to middle-income countries (LMICs), where expert and biopsy services are limited.
AI.method: Multi-task learning approach combining image-level classification and lesion segmentation on acid stain colposcopy images, trained with extensive data augmentation.
AI.result: Outperformed medical experts on in-distribution validation (Balanced Accuracy: 0.68 vs 0.64). External validation across four countries showed AUC ranging from 0.54 to 0.80. Performance affected by age, transformation zone, comorbidities, and pathognomonic signs.
AI.conclusion: First DL-based cervical cancer screening validated on multi-country data. While promising, model robustness and generalizability need improvement due to high cross-country variability and negative impact of comorbidities.

title: Gaussian Spatial Priors for Anatomy-Aware Object Detection in Surgical Videos
summary: Detecting anatomical structures in surgical video is essential for intraoperative safety frameworks such as the Critical View of Myopectineal Orifice (CVMPO) in inguinal hernia repair. While prominent structures like the Cooper's Ligament and Triangle of Doom are reliably detected by standard methods, smaller structures such as the epigastric vessels remain challenging due to their visual ambiguity and intermittent visibility. We observe that the spatial relationship between structures is anatomically constrained, and propose a Gaussian Spatial Prior (GSP) module that encodes this relationship as a compact, parametric bias injected into the self-attention of a DAB-DETR decoder. The prior is computed offline from training annotations as a small set of frozen Gaussian parameters and recomputed at each decoder layer using the iteratively refined reference points. On a dataset of inguinal hernia repair videos with 5-fold cross-validation, GSP improves dependent class detection by $+33.5\%$ ($\text{AP}_{50}$) over DAB-DETR and $+53.9\%$ over YOLOv26, while also improving anchor detection by $+6.0\%$. These gains are statistically significant across all folds ($p=0.012$, paired $t-$test).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Gaussian Spatial Prior (GSP) module that encodes anatomical spatial relationships to improve detection of visually ambiguous structures (like epigastric vessels) in surgical video, achieving +33.5% AP50 improvement over DAB-DETR and +53.9% over YOLOv6 on inguinal hernia repair datasets.
AI.motivation: Small, visually ambiguous anatomical structures (e.g., epigastric vessels) are difficult to detect reliably in surgical video using standard methods, yet their identification is critical for intraoperative safety frameworks like CVMPO.
AI.method: A Gaussian Spatial Prior (GSP) module that encodes anatomically constrained spatial relationships as parametric biases injected into DAB-DETR's self-attention. The prior is computed offline from training annotations as frozen Gaussian parameters and updated per decoder layer using iteratively refined reference points.
AI.result: GSP improves dependent class detection by +33.5% (AP50) over DAB-DETR and +53.9% over YOLOv6, and anchor detection by +6.0%. Results are statistically significant (p=0.012) across 5-fold cross-validation.
AI.conclusion: Encoding anatomical spatial priors via Gaussian parameters in transformer attention significantly boosts detection of visually challenging structures in surgical video, offering a practical solution for intraoperative safety.

title: Bridging Geographic Bias in Urban Streetscape Inference via Lifelong Learning with Visual-Semantic Pivoting
summary: Visual perception of urban streetscapes underpins evidence-based decisions in landscape planning, public health, and place-making. Yet models trained on a few well-photographed metropolises systematically misjudge underrepresented districts, propagating geographic bias into downstream policy. We address this gap with HVSP-LL, a lifelong learning framework that couples a stratified visual-semantic pivoting module with an equity-aware rehearsal mechanism. The pivoting module organises landscape concepts along a three-tier ontology (macro structure, meso composition, micro element) and aligns image features to learnable semantic anchors at each tier, providing transferable representations that resist distributional drift. The lifelong adaptation component sequentially absorbs new urban regions while constraining inter-region perception gaps through a worst-region sample-reweighting objective and a structurally-aware exemplar buffer. We evaluate HVSP-LL on a panoramic streetscape benchmark assembled from twelve cities across four continents and seven perceptual dimensions. The framework attains 0.834 Spearman correlation on the held-out city sequence, an absolute 6.1 point improvement over the strongest continual baseline, and shrinks the inter-city perception gap to 0.094 -- a 38% reduction relative to the strongest continual baseline (0.151) and a 57% reduction relative to a representative regularisation baseline (0.218). Ablations confirm that each tier of the pivoting hierarchy contributes monotonically, and the equity-aware rehearsal converts mean backward transfer from -0.038 (without retention) to +0.013, eliminating catastrophic forgetting on the held-out sequence. Our results indicate that hierarchical anchoring is a practical pathway toward geographically equitable streetscape inference at city scale.

AI.tldr: HVSP-LL is a lifelong learning framework for urban streetscape perception that uses hierarchical semantic anchoring and equity-aware rehearsal to reduce geographic bias, achieving a 38% reduction in inter-city perception gap compared to strongest baselines.
