To enhance efficiency, we introduce an asymmetric attention mask that enables joint training of both experts while allowing the action model to bypass explicit video generation during inference. This design ensures training-inference consistency and significantly reduces computational costs without compromising planning performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on the NAVSIM navhard and navtest benchmarks and the CityWalker navigation benchmark, validating both the generalizability and efficiency across diverse tasks. Real-robot deployments further confirm the practical feasibility of our approach.

AI.tldr: Metis is an end-to-end World Action Model framework that decouples video generation from action prediction using a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture with asymmetric attention masking, achieving state-of-the-art performance on navigation benchmarks while significantly reducing inference latency.
AI.motivation: Existing WAM approaches suffer from high inference latency due to future observation prediction at test time, and tightly coupled video and action modeling leads to representational mismatch and degraded generalization.
AI.method: Metis uses a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture with dedicated experts for video generation and action prediction, plus an asymmetric attention mask enabling joint training while allowing the action model to bypass video generation during inference.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM navhard/navtest benchmarks and CityWalker navigation benchmark, with real-robot deployment validation demonstrating practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling video generation and action prediction with asymmetric attention masking effectively reduces computational costs while maintaining planning performance, validated across diverse tasks and real-world deployment.

title: Deep Residual Injection for Full-Spectrum Forensic Signal Perception in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have been increasingly adopted in forensics for their robust semantic understanding. As AI-generated images become realistic, semantic-level inconsistencies alone are often insufficient for reliable detection. This motivates a critical question: whether MLLMs can achieve full-spectrum forensic signal perception, i.e., capturing low-level generator artifacts without sacrificing pre-trained semantic knowledge. We further perform a layer-wise analysis of forensic signal perception in MLLMs, showing that semantic information is primarily formed in the early-to-middle layers, whereas direct fine-tuning for artifact learning disrupts these semantic representations. Based on this insight, we propose Deep Visual Residual MLLM (Deep-VRM) to preserve early semantic processing while injecting artifact-specific visual signals as a residual path into an intermediate layer, where they are fused with semantic token representations and propagated through subsequent trainable layers. This enables later layers to jointly model semantic reasoning and signal-level forensic cues, and surprisingly, the model learns to adaptively leverage different levels of forensic signals depending on the input, achieving robust and generalizable detection performance. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art across most benchmarks. The code and data are available at https://github.com/KQL11/Deep-VRM.

AI.tldr: Deep-VRM is a multimodal LLM that preserves semantic knowledge while injecting artifact-specific visual signals via residual paths, achieving state-of-the-art AI-generated image detection by adaptively fusing low-level forensic cues with high-level semantic reasoning.
AI.motivation: As AI-generated images become increasingly realistic, semantic-level inconsistencies alone are insufficient for reliable detection. The authors question whether MLLMs can achieve full-spectrum forensic signal perception—capturing low-level generator artifacts without sacrificing pre-trained semantic knowledge.
AI.method: The authors propose Deep Visual Residual MLLM (Deep-VRM), which preserves early semantic processing in early-to-middle layers while injecting artifact-specific visual signals as a residual path into an intermediate layer. These signals are fused with semantic token representations and propagated through subsequent trainable layers, enabling joint modeling of semantic reasoning and signal-level forensic cues.
AI.result: The model learns to adaptively leverage different levels of forensic signals depending on the input, achieving robust and generalizable detection performance. Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art results across most benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Layer-wise analysis shows semantic information forms in early-to-middle layers, and direct fine-tuning disrupts these representations. Deep-VRM's residual path design successfully preserves semantics while enabling artifact learning, leading to robust AI-generated image detection.

title: Text region detection in historical astronomical diagrams
summary: Text detection is a crucial task in the analysis of historical documents. While datasets and benchmarks exist for text detection in manuscripts and maps, the study of text in mathematical diagrams has received little attention. To address this, we introduce a large-scale, diverse, open-access dataset of 948 historical astronomical diagrams containing 10,940 oriented polygonal text regions. Our dataset spans ten centuries (8th to 18th) and seven main linguistic traditions: Arabic and Persian (115), Chinese (332), Byzantine (233), Latin (185), Hebrew (48), and Sanskrit (35). It captures a wide range of diagram styles and textual content, from symbols to multi-line paragraphs. Each text instance is annotated with ordered polygons that precisely delineate text regions and encode the reading direction. In addition, we annotated the 2,293 regions in Latin diagrams with 20 class labels. We evaluated several strong baselines on our dataset, including TESTR, DeepSolo++, and Poly-DETR, a simple extension of DINO-DETR that we design to predict ordered polygon vertices. Poly-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MTHv2 and cBAD2019 benchmarks and provides a solid, simple baseline on our dataset. Code and dataset available online.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a large-scale, open-access dataset of 948 historical astronomical diagrams with 10,940 annotated polygonal text regions spanning ten centuries and seven linguistic traditions, along with Poly-DETR—a simple extension of DINO-DETR for ordered polygon prediction—that achieves state-of-the-art results on existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Text detection in historical mathematical diagrams has received little attention despite its importance; existing datasets focus on manuscripts and maps, leaving a gap in studying text in astronomical diagrams.
AI.method: The authors created a dataset of 948 historical astronomical diagrams (8th–18th centuries, 7 languages) with 10,940 oriented polygonal text regions annotated with reading direction. They also designed Poly-DETR, a simple extension of DINO-DETR, to predict ordered polygon vertices for text detection.
AI.result: Poly-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance on MTHv2 and cBAD2019 benchmarks and provides a solid baseline on their new dataset.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and Poly-DETR address the understudied problem of text detection in historical astronomical diagrams, providing resources for future research.

title: SiGnature: Explicit Motion Diffusion for Stylized Semantic Gesture
summary: While recent advances in co-speech gesture generation have achieved impressive rhythmic synchronization, synthesizing gestures that are both semantically meaningful and faithful to a speaker's unique non-verbal style remains an open challenge. Semantic gestures, such as iconic shapes or deictic pointing, are statistically sparse, making them difficult to learn effectively within standard generative models. We present SiGnature, a framework for Stylized and Semantic Gesture generation that reconciles precise semantic control with high-fidelity style preservation. Unlike prevalent methods that rely on entangled latent representations, SiGnature operates in an explicit joint-rotation space. This design enables our core contribution, Joint Motion Integration (JMI), a training-free inference mechanism capable of injecting any external motion sequence, particularly in-the-wild semantic gestures, directly into the diffusion process. JMI automatically identifies the specific ``active joints'' conveying a semantic action and injects them into the generation, while relying on the diffusion backbone to synthesize the remaining body dynamics, including posture and flow, in accordance with the pre-learned style of the target speaker. This allows for the plug-and-play integration of arbitrary motions, including complex semantic gestures, without retraining or introducing the ``Frankenstein'' artifacts typical of cut-and-paste methods. Extensive experiments and perceptual studies demonstrate that SiGnature offers superior semantic motion control while maintaining smooth and natural co-speech gesture generation and preserving the distinct characteristics of the speaker, thereby outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SiGnature is a framework for co-speech gesture generation that achieves both semantic meaning and speaker-specific style preservation through a training-free Joint Motion Integration (JMI) mechanism operating in explicit joint-rotation space, enabling plug-and-play injection of external semantic gestures without retraining or artifacts.
AI.motivation: Existing co-speech gesture generation methods achieve good rhythmic synchronization but fail to produce semantically meaningful gestures (e.g., iconic shapes, pointing) while preserving a speaker's unique non-verbal style, because semantic gestures are statistically sparse and hard to learn in standard generative models with entangled latent representations.
AI.method: SiGnature operates in explicit joint-rotation space and introduces Joint Motion Integration (JMI), a training-free inference mechanism that automatically identifies "active joints" conveying a semantic action from an external motion sequence and injects them into the diffusion process, while the backbone synthesizes remaining body dynamics in accordance with the pre-learned speaker style.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and perceptual studies show SiGnature offers superior semantic motion control while maintaining smooth, natural co-speech gestures and preserving distinct speaker characteristics, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines without retraining or Frankenstein artifacts.
AI.conclusion: SiGnature effectively reconciles the long-standing tension between semantic gesture control and style preservation in co-speech gesture generation through its training-free JMI mechanism in explicit joint space, enabling plug-and-play integration of arbitrary semantic motions.

title: High-Fidelity 4D Hand-Object Capture via Multi-View Spatiotemporal Tracking and Physics-Aware Gaussians
summary: The growing demand for high-fidelity 4D hand-object interaction (HOI) data in embodied AI and spatial computing is currently bottlenecked by the reliance on pre-scanned object templates and physical markers. While recent methods have demonstrated promising results in reconstructing 4D hand-object interaction from videos, they are highly sensitive to initial estimates of hand and object poses. Yet, estimating these poses from images is challenging, in particular under severe occlusion which is inherent in hand-object interaction scenarios. We propose a novel system for the robust and accurate reconstruction of hands and objects from synchronized and calibrated multi-view videos without requiring any templates or markers. Our system consists of two main components with key innovations: (1) a multi-view feed-forward transformer model that aggregates cross-view geometry and temporal cues to provide a reliable, metric-consistent initialization for both poses and dense object geometry, and (2) a hand-object physics-aware Gaussian-based optimization framework to refine the initial estimates, integrating tetrahedral constraints, collision refinement, and appearance decomposition to produce physically plausible and visually accurate reconstruction. Validated on public benchmarks and an extensive internal dataset, our pipeline achieves highly robust, artifact-free reconstruction, providing an efficient foundation for automated 4D asset generation. Our project page are available at https://zyshen021.github.io/HOSTPG/.

AI.tldr: A template-free, markerless system for robust 4D hand-object interaction reconstruction from multi-view videos using a feed-forward transformer for initialization and a physics-aware Gaussian optimization for refinement.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D hand-object interaction reconstruction relies on pre-scanned object templates and physical markers, and current methods are highly sensitive to initial pose estimates which are difficult under severe occlusion.
AI.method: (1) Multi-view feed-forward transformer aggregating cross-view geometry and temporal cues for robust initialization; (2) Physics-aware Gaussian optimization with tetrahedral constraints, collision refinement, and appearance decomposition.
AI.result: Validated on public benchmarks and internal dataset, achieving robust, artifact-free reconstruction without templates or markers.
AI.conclusion: Provides an efficient, automated foundation for 4D asset generation in embodied AI and spatial computing.

title: OmniOPSD: Rationale-Privileged On-Policy Self-Distillation for Affective Computing
summary: Reinforcement learning for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is often hindered by severe reward sparsity in complex reasoning tasks. This challenge is particularly pronounced in human-centered scenarios involving states, emotions, intentions, and behaviors, where heterogeneous multimodal signals and subjective human factors make high-quality chain-of-thought (CoT) annotations expensive and difficult to obtain. Although many multimodal datasets provide expert-annotated ground-truth labels, directly using these labels for supervised fine-tuning may encourage shortcut learning in multimodal perception and provides limited transparency for safety-critical human--AI interaction. To address these limitations, we propose OmniOPSD, a Rationale-Privileged On-Policy Self-Distillation framework that uses frontier-generated rationales as teacher-side privileged evidence rather than student imitation targets. OmniOPSD uses frontier-generated evidence-aware rationales only as training-time privileged evidence context for a local teacher. The student samples its own rollout from the original multimodal input, while the rationale-privileged teacher scores the same tokens and provides dense token-level supervision. Thus, the student learns on its own trajectory distribution without directly imitating frontier-model completions, and inference requires no labels, rationales, CoT annotations, or closed-source model access. Experiments on MER-UniBench show that OmniOPSD achieves state-of-the-art performance with an average score of $84.19$, and ablations further support the value of rationale-privileged teacher guidance.

AI.tldr: OmniOPSD is a framework that uses frontier-model-generated rationales as privileged evidence for a teacher model to provide dense token-level supervision, enabling student MLLMs to learn without direct imitation of frontier models or expensive annotations. It achieves SOTA performance (84.19) on MER-UniBench.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for MLLMs faces severe reward sparsity in complex reasoning tasks, especially human-centered scenarios involving heterogeneous multimodal signals and subjective human factors. High-quality CoT annotations are expensive, and using ground-truth labels for SFT encourages shortcut learning with limited transparency.
AI.method: OmniOPSD uses frontier-generated evidence-aware rationales as privileged information for a local teacher model during training. The student samples rollouts from original multimodal inputs, while the rationale-privileged teacher scores tokens and provides dense token-level supervision. This avoids direct imitation of frontier-model completions.
AI.result: OmniOPSD achieves state-of-the-art performance with an average score of 84.19 on MER-UniBench. Ablation studies confirm the value of rationale-privileged teacher guidance.
AI.conclusion: The rationale-privileged on-policy self-distillation approach effectively addresses reward sparsity and shortcut learning issues in multimodal reasoning. Inference requires no labels, rationales, CoT annotations, or closed-source model access, making it practical for real-world deployment.

title: TurboGS: Accelerating 3D Gaussian Splatting via Error-Guided Sparse Pixel Sampling and Optimization
comment: Accepted by ICML2026. Project page: https://zhengdong.site/projects/TurboGS/
summary: Consumer-level applications require fast optimization of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with high-fidelity novel view rendering. However, existing 3DGS acceleration approaches still incur substantial computation on redundant pixels while sacrificing fine details. In this paper, we present TurboGS, an error-guided training framework that accelerates 3DGS by concentrating optimization on perceptually informative pixels. TurboGS is built upon four core components: (1) a tile-wise sparse pixel sampling, which, driven by multi-view reconstruction errors during training, prioritizes challenging regions and skips well-reconstructed ones to avoid redundant gradient computation; (2) a tile-wise structure-aware loss with sparse Normalized Cross-Correlation, which provides sparse yet effective supervision to preserve fine details and stabilize training; (3) an error-driven Gaussian density control strategy, which dynamically allocates model capacity and removes redundant primitives; and (4) a tailored hybrid optimizer that couples Hessian-informed updates with Adam moment damping to stabilize and improve convergence under sparse supervision. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that TurboGS can deliver on par or superior rendering quality within 100 seconds on a single RTX 5090 GPU card (up to 10x training speedup over vanilla 3DGS).

AI.tldr: TurboGS accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting training by focusing optimization on perceptually informative pixels, achieving up to 10x speedup over vanilla 3DGS with comparable or better rendering quality in as little as 100 seconds on a single RTX 5090 GPU.
AI.motivation: Consumer-level applications need fast optimization of 3DGS with high-fidelity rendering, but existing acceleration methods waste computation on redundant pixels and lose fine details.
AI.method: Four components: (1) tile-wise sparse pixel sampling guided by multi-view reconstruction errors; (2) tile-wise structure-aware loss with sparse Normalized Cross-Correlation; (3) error-driven Gaussian density control; (4) hybrid optimizer combining Hessian-informed updates with Adam moment damping.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks, TurboGS achieves on par or superior rendering quality within 100 seconds on a single RTX 5090 GPU, delivering up to 10x training speedup over vanilla 3DGS.
AI.conclusion: TurboGS effectively accelerates 3DGS training by focusing computation on challenging regions, preserving fine details, and stabilizing convergence, making it suitable for consumer-level applications.

title: GOOSE-M2F: Adapting Mask2Former for High-Fidelity, Long-Tailed Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation in Unstructured Outdoor Terrain
comment: This solution has got 3rd position at GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation (FGSS) Challenge at ICRA~2026
summary: We present GOOSE-M2F, a task-specific adaptation of Mask2Former for the GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation (FGSS) Challenge at ICRA~2026. The GOOSE benchmark spans 64 fine-grained classes across unstructured outdoor terrain with a severely long-tailed distribution, where rare classes occupy fewer than 50 pixels per image. We extend the Swin-Large Mask2Former baseline with three targeted contributions: (1)200 Object Queries to eliminate representational saturation; (2)a Feature Refinement Module (FRM) combining ASPP-lite and CBAM dual-attention; and (3)an Auxiliary Supervision Head that delivers direct per-pixel gradients for rare classes. A multi-stage training strategy pairs Distribution-Balanced loss, Rare-Class Copy-Paste augmentation, dynamic IoU-aware re-weighting, and EMA. At inference, a dense sliding-window engine with 2D Gaussian kernel blending and 4-scale TTA adds +10.57\%. GOOSE-M2F achieves 70.08\% Official Composite mIoU (63.55\% fine, 76.61\% coarse), placing 3rd on the GOOSE 2D FGSS leaderboard. Code and trained models are publicly available at: \href{https://github.com/Aditya-Lingam-9000/GOOSE-M2F}{Github GOOSE-M2F Code} and \href{https://huggingface.co/XYZ9843/GOOSE-M2F}{Hugging Face GOOSE-M2F}.

AI.tldr: GOOSE-M2F is a task-specific adaptation of Mask2Former that achieves 3rd place on the GOOSE 2D FGSS leaderboard (70.08% Composite mIoU) by addressing long-tailed distribution in unstructured outdoor terrain segmentation through 200 object queries, a Feature Refinement Module (ASPP-lite + CBAM), auxiliary supervision, multi-stage training strategies, and dense sliding-window inference with TTA.
AI.motivation: To address the GOOSE benchmark's challenge of 64 fine-grained classes with severely long-tailed distribution (rare classes under 50 pixels/image), which causes representational saturation and poor rare-class performance in standard Mask2Former.
AI.method: Extends Swin-Large Mask2Former with: (1) 200 Object Queries to prevent representational saturation; (2) Feature Refinement Module (FRM) combining ASPP-lite and CBAM dual-attention; (3) Auxiliary Supervision Head for direct per-pixel gradients on rare classes. Multi-stage training uses Distribution-Balanced loss, Rare-Class Copy-Paste, dynamic IoU-aware re-weighting, and EMA. Inference employs dense sliding-window with 2D Gaussian kernel blending and 4-scale TTA.
AI.result: Achieves 70.08% Official Composite mIoU (63.55% fine, 76.61% coarse), placing 3rd on the GOOSE 2D FGSS leaderboard. Dense sliding-window inference with TTA adds +10.57% improvement.
AI.conclusion: GOOSE-M2F demonstrates that targeted architectural modifications (increased queries, feature refinement, auxiliary supervision) combined with careful training strategies effectively mitigate long-tail distribution challenges in fine-grained semantic segmentation for unstructured outdoor environments.

title: Learning Directional Semantic Transitions for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Analysis
summary: Chest X-ray (CXR) interpretation often requires longitudinal comparison to assess disease progression. Existing approaches typically rely on temporal feature fusion or inter-study discrepancy modeling, yet remain limited in capturing subtle progression semantics and overlook the inherently directional nature of disease trajectories. In this paper, we propose ProTrans, a novel vision-language pretraining framework that formulates disease progression as a directional semantic transition between paired CXR studies. ProTrans leverages radiology reports to anchor individual CXR representations within interpretable disease states, and introduces a learnable progression feature map to explicitly encode semantic shifts between states, aligned with report-derived progression descriptions. To enforce direction-aware perception, ProTrans incorporates a reversed temporal modeling process and imposes bidirectional reconstruction consistency across states and transitions, thereby disentangling directional semantics and promoting coherent trajectory modeling. Extensive experiments on longitudinal downstream tasks, including disease progression classification and progression captioning, demonstrate that ProTrans consistently outperforms existing methods, establishing a unified pretraining framework for longitudinal CXR understanding. https://github.com/RPIDIAL/ProTrans

AI.tldr: ProTrans is a vision-language pretraining framework that models disease progression as directional semantic transitions between paired chest X-rays, using radiology reports to anchor representations and outperforming existing methods on longitudinal analysis tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing CXR longitudinal analysis methods struggle to capture subtle progression semantics and overlook the inherently directional nature of disease trajectories.
AI.method: ProTrans formulates disease progression as directional semantic transitions between paired CXR studies, uses radiology reports to anchor individual CXR representations in disease states, introduces a learnable progression feature map for semantic shifts, incorporates reversed temporal modeling, and imposes bidirectional reconstruction consistency.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods consistently on disease progression classification and progression captioning tasks.
AI.conclusion: ProTrans establishes a unified pretraining framework for longitudinal CXR understanding by explicitly encoding directional disease trajectories.

title: You Don't Need Strong Assumptions: Visual Representation Learning via Temporal Differences
summary: Progress in AI has largely been driven by methods that assume less. As compute and data increase, approaches with weaker inductive biases generally outperform those with stronger assumptions. This is particularly characteristic of the field of Visual Representation Learning, where approaches have gone from being dominated by Supervised Learning, to Weakly Supervised Learning, to the now widespread success of Self-Supervised Learning without human labels. Yet, even modern Self-Supervised Learning approaches still depend on strong inductive biases such as augmentations, masking, or cropping. If this trend holds, even these remaining biases should become bottlenecks at scale -- and our experiments confirm this: the optimal strength of inductive biases decreases as data grows. This motivates the search for approaches that rely on fewer assumptions. To this end, we introduce Temporal Difference in Vision (TDV), a new paradigm for self-supervised learning from video that avoids existing inductive biases, relying instead on a causal assumption that the past causes the future. TDV functions by jointly training an image encoder and a motion encoder so that the current frame's representation plus the encoded motion equals the next frame's representation. Despite not leveraging any strong inductive biases, TDV matches state-of-the-art recipes on dense spatial tasks, laying the foundation for representation learning without strong assumptions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that as data scales up, strong inductive biases become bottlenecks, and introduces TDV (Temporal Difference in Vision) - a self-supervised learning method from video that avoids common biases by using only a causal assumption (past causes future), achieving competitive results on dense spatial tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper observes a trend in visual representation learning where methods with weaker inductive biases outperform those with stronger assumptions as data/compute scales. The authors hypothesize that even modern SSL biases (augmentations, masking, cropping) will become bottlenecks at scale, motivating the search for approaches with fewer assumptions.
AI.method: TDV jointly trains an image encoder and a motion encoder such that the current frame's representation plus the encoded motion equals the next frame's representation. It only assumes a causal relationship (past causes future) and avoids augmentations, masking, or cropping biases.
AI.result: Experiments confirm that optimal inductive bias strength decreases as data grows. Despite having no strong inductive biases, TDV matches state-of-the-art results on dense spatial tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper lays the foundation for representation learning without strong assumptions, suggesting that causal assumptions from video may be sufficient for effective visual learning at scale.

title: VEPHand: View-Efficient Photometric Hand Performance Capture at Scale
summary: Robust, high-fidelity 3D hand capture, while fundamental to digital human creation, remains challenging with practical multi-view systems that balance rich photometry with the geometric ambiguities of reconstruction arising from limited viewpoint density. This paper presents an end-to-end pipeline for dynamic hand performance capture and registration, specifically designed for view-efficient setups ($\sim$20 views). We address key challenges with two primary innovations. First, to overcome reconstruction difficulties like limited view overlap and background clutter, our mask-free neural method robustly extracts detailed hand geometry and appearance from unmasked images using scene parameterization and scenario-specific density regularization. Second, addressing registration challenges such as accurately capturing non-linear skin deformations and ensuring plausible results during severe self-contact, we propose a physics-inspired framework. It aligns reconstructions to a personalized hand model by optimizing intrinsic volumetric offsets within its canonical tetrahedral mesh, alongside pose parameters. This approach, supported by robust losses and optimization, captures fine surface deformations, ensures plausible results under severe articulation and self-contact, and demonstrates strong tolerance to input noise. We demonstrate the scalability and robustness of our automated pipeline on an extensive dataset of over 12,000 sequences, from which we also derive a large-scale, high-quality synthetic 2D/3D hand dataset for training downstream tasks. This showcases its effectiveness for single hands, intricate two-hand interactions, and natural hand-object manipulations. Our method achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity in view-efficient, unmasked scenarios and highly accurate registration. Our project page are available at https://zyshen021.github.io/VEPHand/.

AI.tldr: Presents VEPHand, an end-to-end pipeline for dynamic 3D hand capture from limited multi-view setups (~20 views), featuring mask-free neural reconstruction and physics-inspired registration for high-fidelity hand modeling.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge of reconstructing high-fidelity 3D hands from practical multi-view systems with limited viewpoint density, addressing issues like geometric ambiguity, limited view overlap, background clutter, and non-linear skin deformations under self-contact.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) A mask-free neural reconstruction method using scene parameterization and scenario-specific density regularization to extract hand geometry/appearance from unmasked images. (2) A physics-inspired registration framework that aligns reconstructions to a personalized hand model via intrinsic volumetric offsets within a canonical tetrahedral mesh and pose optimization, supported by robust losses.
AI.result: State-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity in view-efficient, unmasked scenarios with highly accurate registration. Validated on over 12,000 sequences including single hands, two-hand interactions, and hand-object manipulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed automated pipeline is scalable, robust, and achieves top-tier performance for dynamic hand capture in practical multi-view setups. A large-scale synthetic dataset derived from this work also supports downstream tasks.

title: CRIS: Cross-Plane Self-Supervised Isotropic Restoration for Anisotropic Volumetric Imaging Across Modalities
comment: 22 pages, 8 figures, supplementary material included. Submitted to Medical Image Analysis
summary: Anisotropic volumetric acquisitions are common in clinical MRI and volume electron microscopy (vEM), where sparse through-plane sampling creates thick slices or sections that degrade orthogonal reformats and downstream analysis. We present CRIS, a cross-plane self-supervised framework for isotropic restoration without paired isotropic ground truth. CRIS casts 3D restoration as 2D stripe completion on orthogonal reformats of an isotropic grid: high-resolution in-plane slices are synthetically degraded and periodically masked for training, while at inference blank slices define the isotropic grid, two orthogonal reformats are restored, and predictions are fused by multi-view averaging. We evaluate CRIS on two MRI cohorts and two microscopy benchmarks up to 8x anisotropy. On brain MRI, CRIS achieves 32.921 +/- 0.436 dB PSNR and 0.9631 +/- 0.0027 SSIM, outperforming interpolation, SMORE4, SIMPLE, SA-INR, and ATME, and gives the best segmentation consistency (Dice 0.940 +/- 0.004, ASSD 0.245 +/- 0.014 mm, HD99 1.275 +/- 0.061 mm). On reference-free abdominal MRI, CRIS reduces FID/KID to 48.714/0.023. On vEM, CRIS outperforms interpolation, NIIV, and vEMINR, reaching 29.133 dB/0.834 3D PSNR/SSIM at 4x, 27.123 dB/0.734 on EPFL at 8x, and 21.915 dB/0.699 on noisy hemibrain data. In a robustness experiment, one variable-gap CRIS model evaluated across gap factors 3--7 and coronal, axial, and sagittal degradations maintained higher PSNR/SSIM than interpolation (36.36--31.14 dB and 0.977--0.932 vs. 33.07--27.85 dB and 0.951--0.853). These results support CRIS as a modality-flexible route to isotropic restoration without paired isotropic targets or configuration-specific retraining. Code is available at https://github.com/adi-hatav/CRIS.

AI.tldr: CRIS is a self-supervised framework for restoring isotropic resolution from anisotropic volumetric data (MRI and electron microscopy) without requiring paired isotropic ground truth. It works by treating 3D restoration as 2D stripe completion on orthogonal reformats, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Anisotropic volumetric acquisitions (e.g., thick slices in clinical MRI and volume electron microscopy) degrade orthogonal view reformats and downstream analysis. Existing methods typically require paired isotropic ground truth or are modality-specific, limiting their applicability.
AI.method: CRIS casts 3D isotropic restoration as a 2D stripe completion problem on orthogonal reformats of an isotropic grid. During training, high-resolution in-plane slices are synthetically degraded and periodically masked. At inference, blank slices define the isotropic grid, two orthogonal reformats are restored independently, and predictions are fused via multi-view averaging.
AI.result: On brain MRI: 32.921 dB PSNR, 0.9631 SSIM, best segmentation consistency (Dice 0.940). On abdominal MRI (reference-free): FID 48.714, KID 0.023. On vEM: outperforms baselines at 4x and 8x anisotropy. Robustness experiment: single model handles gap factors 3-7 across coronal/axial/sagittal degradations, outperforming interpolation (36.36-31.14 dB vs 33.07-27.85 dB PSNR).
AI.conclusion: CRIS provides a modality-flexible, self-supervised route to isotropic restoration that does not require paired isotropic targets or configuration-specific retraining, with code publicly available.

title: HadBalance: A Plug-and-Play Unified Global Geometric Prior Framework for Generalizable Biomedical Segmentation
comment: Provisionally accepted by the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention (MICCAI 2026). 11 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables
summary: Precise biomedical image segmentation is crucial for clinical diagnosis. Geometric cues (e.g., boundary, shape, and topology) can improve structural consistency, yet most are task-specific and lack a unified geometric foundation that generalizes across organs and modalities. We are motivated by the observation that several medical segmentation targets can be approximated as globally near-convex shapes. A convex region is one in which any two interior points can be connected by a line segment entirely contained within the region. In practice, medical targets may exhibit small local concavities or boundary irregularities; we refer to such globally convex-like shapes as near-convex. Motivated by this, we derive Hadwiger Shape Priors from Hadwiger's theorem as an interpretable global regularizer using three 2D measures: area A, perimeter P, and Euler characteristic chi, enabling transfer across organs and modalities. However, because medical datasets are shape-heterogeneous, enforcing near-convex priors uniformly can over-regularize non-convex anatomy with significant concavities, washing out concavities and fine details and degrading segmentation accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose Conflict-Aware Objective Balancing (CAOB), which integrates shape priors with segmentation in a gradient-aware manner. For each prior, CAOB removes only the gradient component that conflicts with segmentation while preserving the remaining aligned component, and adaptively regulates objective influences to prevent prior dominance. This enables stable use of shape priors on shape-heterogeneous data without erasing genuine concavities or fine structural details. We call this plug-and-play framework HadBalance.

AI.tldr: HadBalance integrates Hadwiger Shape Priors (area, perimeter, Euler characteristic) as a global regularizer for near-convex medical image segmentation, paired with Conflict-Aware Objective Balancing (CAOB) to prevent over-regularization on non-convex anatomy, enabling shape-heterogeneous, multi-organ, multi-modal use.
AI.motivation: Existing geometric priors for medical segmentation are task-specific and lack a unified foundation; many targets are near-convex but datasets are shape-heterogeneous, causing uniform convex priors to over-regularize non-convex structures and wash out fine details.
AI.method: Derives Hadwiger Shape Priors (area, perimeter, Euler characteristic) from Hadwiger's theorem as a global regularizer; proposes Conflict-Aware Objective Balancing (CAOB) that removes only gradient components conflicting with segmentation while preserving aligned ones, adaptively regulating prior influence to avoid dominance.
AI.result: Enables stable use of shape priors across shape-heterogeneous data without erasing genuine concavities or fine structural details, improving segmentation accuracy compared to uniform prior enforcement.
AI.conclusion: HadBalance is a plug-and-play framework that generalizes across organs and modalities by combining interpretable geometric priors with gradient-aware conflict balancing.

title: Mind the Gap: Diagnosing Constraint Discovery Failures in Text-in-Image Editing
summary: A key challenge in multimodal reasoning is determining which visual dependencies become relevant under a specific task, rather than merely recognizing visible content. We study this through edit-induced constraint discovery in text-in-image editing, a controlled diagnostic setting where a local text change can activate secondary consistency constraints: given a valid editing instruction and an image, can a model identify the secondary regions that must also change? Across 461 diagnostic cases, four MLLMs, and 19 constraint subtypes, models recover only 46% case-level macro recall under unguided prompting versus 94% when constraints are explicitly provided, suggesting that a substantial portion of the failure arises when models must decide which unstated dependencies to surface. Oracle-field decomposition shows that case-specific causal explanations are the most effective partial guidance (0.782 recall), above region names (0.610) or type labels (0.646), suggesting that edit-specific causal cues account for much of the oracle gain. A downstream experiment further shows that higher self-discovery recall does not necessarily improve task performance: unverified self-discovery introduces false positives that offset recall gains, motivating precision-aware constraint elicitation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how multimodal LLMs struggle with identifying secondary visual constraints triggered by local text edits in images. Models achieve only 46% recall under unguided prompting versus 94% with explicit constraints, showing failures stem from deciding which dependencies to surface. Causal explanations are the most effective guidance, and higher self-discovery recall doesn't guarantee better downstream task performance due to false positives.
AI.motivation: To study whether multimodal reasoning models can determine which visual dependencies become relevant under a specific task (not just recognize visible content), using edit-induced constraint discovery in text-in-image editing as a controlled diagnostic setting.
AI.method: Created 461 diagnostic cases with 19 constraint subtypes across 4 MLLMs. Used oracle-field decomposition to compare different guidance types (causal explanations, region names, type labels). Conducted downstream experiments to evaluate precision-aware constraint elicitation.
AI.result: Unguided prompting yields only 46% case-level macro recall vs 94% with explicit constraints. Causal explanations achieve 0.782 recall (best partial guidance), above region names (0.610) and type labels (0.646). Higher self-discovery recall does not necessarily improve downstream task performance due to false positives.
AI.conclusion: A substantial portion of multimodal reasoning failures arise when models must decide which unstated dependencies to surface, not just from recognizing visible content. Precision-aware constraint elicitation is needed, as unverified self-discovery can introduce harmful false positives.

title: A Text Recognition Dataset from Sahidic Coptic Ancient Manuscripts
summary: In this work, we target Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) in low-resource scenarios, which arise from underrepresented languages, rare scripts, and degraded visual conditions typical of historical documents. We introduce SCAM (Sahidic Coptic Ancient Manuscripts), a new line-level dataset built from digitized ancient manuscripts written in the extinct Sahidic Coptic dialect. The dataset reflects a realistic and challenging setting, as it combines heterogeneous acquisition conditions across libraries with typical manuscript degradations such as ink fading, bleed-through, and material deterioration. In addition to visual complexity, SCAM poses significant linguistic challenges due to the scarcity of resources for Sahidic Coptic, its uncommon alphabet, and dialect-specific diacritics. To support research in low-resource HTR, we benchmark several state-of-the-art approaches based on different paradigms, highlighting their limitations and strengths in this setting. Our results underline the gap between current HTR performance on well-resourced modern scripts and historically grounded, low-resource scenarios, thus providing a reference point for future developments.

AI.tldr: SCAM is a new line-level handwritten text recognition dataset for the extinct Sahidic Coptic language, featuring realistic historical document challenges (degradations, low-resource language). Benchmarking shows a significant performance gap versus well-resourced modern scripts.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of datasets for handwritten text recognition in low-resource scenarios, particularly for extinct languages like Sahidic Coptic with scarce linguistic resources, uncommon alphabets, and degraded historical manuscripts.
AI.method: Created SCAM dataset from digitized ancient Sahidic Coptic manuscripts across libraries; benchmarked multiple state-of-the-art HTR approaches from different paradigms on this dataset.
AI.result: Identified a clear performance gap between current HTR systems on well-resourced modern scripts versus low-resource historical scenarios; highlighted limitations and strengths of existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: SCAM provides a realistic benchmark for low-resource HTR research; substantial progress is still needed to bridge the performance gap with well-resourced modern scripts.

title: Multi-Task Tennis Stroke Biomechanics Analysis Using MediaPipe Pose
summary: We built a multi-task pipeline for tennis stroke biomechanics from plain RGB video. On top of pose-based stroke recognition, it adds two new tasks, predicting shot direction and grading posture quality, plus a rule-based feedback layer that suggests coaching tips. Strokes are found automatically using a weighted joint velocity score, s(t) = 0.5 v_wrist + 0.3 m_elbow + 0.2 m_shoulder, removing the need for manual annotation. Pose comes from MediaPipe Pose Landmarker (33 landmarks, metric world coordinates), with each stroke turned into a 30-frame by 39-feature sequence for TennisTransformerGPU, a compact 564,103-parameter transformer (4 layers, 4 heads, d=128) with three parallel output heads. Trained on 1,281 labeled strokes from 7 pros and 1 amateur across 11 videos, it hits 83.7% stroke-type accuracy, 61.9% on direction, and 62.6% on posture under a random 80/20 split. The interesting test is cross-player: train on pros, evaluate on the amateur. Stroke type barely budges, 82.9%, a 0.8% drop. Direction prediction does not transfer; it just falls back to the majority class. An ablation shows why world coordinates matter so much here: switching to image-space landmarks tanks cross-player stroke-type accuracy from 83% to 47% and direction from 68% to 21%. Everything runs on Kaggle's free T4 GPU tier and is fully reproducible.

AI.tldr: A multi-task RGB-video pipeline (TennisTransformerGPU) that recognizes tennis strokes, predicts shot direction, and grades posture quality, achieving 83.7% stroke accuracy but failing to transfer direction across players.
AI.motivation: To automate tennis biomechanics analysis from plain RGB video without manual annotation, adding shot direction and posture quality as new tasks beyond existing stroke recognition.
AI.method: Uses MediaPipe for 3D pose, a weighted joint velocity score for automatic stroke detection, and a compact transformer (564K params, 4 layers, 4 heads, d=128) with three parallel output heads trained on 1,281 labeled strokes.
AI.result: 83.7% stroke-type accuracy, 61.9% direction, 62.6% posture. Cross-player: stroke type drops 0.8% to 82.9%, direction collapses to majority class. World coordinates are critical (image-space drops accuracy from 83% to 47%).
AI.conclusion: Stroke type transfers well across players, but direction prediction does not; world coordinates are essential. The pipeline is reproducible on free T4 GPU.

title: Stringalign: Moving beyond summary statistics with a transparent Unicode-aware tool for evaluating automatic transcription models
summary: Comparing text strings is crucial when evaluating and understanding the performance of various text processing tasks such as document recognition and audio transcription. With an increasingly complex landscape of AI-based handwritten text recognition (HTR), optical character recognition (OCR) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) models, there is a need for tools that facilitate evaluation in a flexible and reproducible way. This paper presents Stringalign, a Python library designed to simplify the evaluation process for automatic transcription projects and facilitate transparent evaluation. Stringalign's tools to examine and visualise both the rate of errors and the types of errors a model makes, give insights into possible improvements and help inform model selection for a particular task. Widely used string comparison metrics, such as the character and word error rates (CER and WER), although useful, can be ambiguous due to varying definitions of what constitutes a character and a word. Stringalign addresses this challenge by ensuring all preprocessing (i.e. normalisation and tokenisation) is transparent and easily replicable, and by providing tools to move beyond summary statistics and analyse common model errors. Moreover, Stringalign adheres to FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) principles for research software while staying lightweight and easy to adapt into researchers existing workflows. In this paper, we discuss challenges with character and word level string comparisons and show through examples that where existing tools can yield opaque and sometimes confusing results, Stringalign provides an easy-to-use and unambiguous alternative.

AI.tldr: Stringalign is a Python library that provides transparent, reproducible, and unambiguous string comparison tools for evaluating text recognition and transcription models, addressing ambiguities in standard metrics like CER and WER.
AI.motivation: Current string comparison metrics (CER, WER) are ambiguous due to inconsistent definitions of characters and words. There is a need for flexible, reproducible evaluation tools that go beyond summary statistics to analyze error types.
AI.method: Stringalign is a Python library that ensures transparent preprocessing (normalization and tokenization), provides tools for visualizing error rates and error types, and adheres to FAIR principles while remaining lightweight.
AI.result: Through examples, Stringalign demonstrates that it provides easy-to-use and unambiguous alternatives compared to existing tools that yield opaque and confusing results.
AI.conclusion: Stringalign facilitates transparent evaluation, gives insights into model improvements, and helps inform model selection for handwriting recognition, OCR, and ASR tasks.

title: The Third Challenge on Image Denoising at NTIRE 2026: Methods and Results
summary: This paper reports on the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Image Denoising, specifically focusing on the high-noise regime ($σ= 50$). The competition investigates advanced neural architectures designed to restore high-fidelity details from images corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). Unlike constrained benchmarks, this track emphasizes peak quantitative performance, measured by Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), without limitations on parameter count or computational overhead. By synthesizing contributions from 20 finalist teams out of 116 registrants, this report benchmarks the latest technical innovations and provides a comprehensive snapshot of the current state-of-the-art in unconstrained image restoration.

AI.tldr: This paper summarizes the NTIRE 2026 Image Denoising Challenge for high-noise (σ=50) conditions, where 20 teams out of 116 registrants competed to maximize PSNR using unconstrained neural architectures.
AI.motivation: To benchmark and advance the state-of-the-art in image denoising under extreme noise conditions without computational constraints, identifying the best-performing architectures.
AI.method: The challenge focused on additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) at σ=50, allowing unlimited parameters and computational overhead, with PSNR as the sole metric.
AI.result: 20 finalist teams out of 116 registrants submitted solutions, showcasing significant innovations in neural architectures for high-noise image restoration.
AI.conclusion: The challenge provides a comprehensive snapshot of the current state-of-the-art in unconstrained image restoration under high-noise conditions.

title: Trusting Right Predictions for Wrong Reasons: A LIME Based Analysis of Deep Learning Interpretability in Lung Cancer Diagnosis
summary: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer-related mortality, with approximately 2.5 million new cases and 1.8 million deaths annually, making reliable diagnosis a clinical priority. Although deep learning models have achieved strong performance in lung cancer classification, evaluation has largely focused on predictive accuracy, leaving their decision-making processes insufficiently examined. This study compares three architecturally distinct models: a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a pretrained ResNet50, and a Vision Transformer (ViT), trained on the IQ-OTH/NCCD lung cancer CT dataset. Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) were applied to investigate model reasoning. In addition to standard performance metrics, a dual-correlation framework was introduced to measure both prediction agreement and explanation agreement across model pairs. All three models achieved strong classification performance, with ResNet50 attaining 98.61% accuracy, CNN 97.91%, and ViT 93.75%, while all achieved ROC-AUC scores of 0.99. Prediction correlations exceeded 0.99 across all model pairs, indicating highly consistent outputs. However, LIME explanation correlations remained below 0.26, revealing substantial differences in the image regions used to reach those predictions. Analysis of misclassified samples further identified a consistent spatial pattern: incorrect predictions were associated with attention outside the lung parenchyma, whereas correct predictions focused primarily within lung regions. These findings demonstrate that prediction agreement is a poor proxy for reasoning consistency, and that interpretability evaluation must be treated as an independent validation criterion alongside predictive performance in clinical AI systems.

AI.tldr: Three deep learning models (CNN, ResNet50, ViT) achieved near-perfect prediction agreement (r>0.99) on lung cancer CT classification but had very low LIME explanation correlations (r<0.26), revealing that models use different image regions despite reaching the same predictions. Misclassifications consistently involved attention outside lung parenchyma.
AI.motivation: Lung cancer diagnosis is critical, but deep learning models are evaluated almost exclusively on predictive accuracy, leaving their decision-making processes insufficiently examined.
AI.method: Three models (CNN, ResNet50, ViT) were trained on the IQ-OTH/NCCD lung cancer CT dataset. LIME was applied for interpretability. A dual-correlation framework measured both prediction agreement and explanation agreement across model pairs.
AI.result: All models achieved strong accuracy (ResNet50: 98.61%, CNN: 97.91%, ViT: 93.75%) with ROC-AUC of 0.99. Prediction correlations >0.99, but explanation correlations <0.26. Misclassifications showed attention outside lung parenchyma.
AI.conclusion: Prediction agreement is a poor proxy for reasoning consistency; interpretability evaluation must be an independent validation criterion in clinical AI systems.

title: PointDiffusion: Diffusion-Based Scene Completion in the Point Cloud Domain
summary: Reconstructing dense 3D scenes from sparse LiDAR point clouds is a fundamental challenge in autonomous driving, where latent diffusion models offer a promising solution. However, existing approaches rely on object-level autoencoders that collapse into unstable global representations at outdoor scale and suffer from ground truth data corrupted by odometry drift that systematically degrades supervision quality. Furthermore, multi-step diffusion inference incurs prohibitive latency for real-time deployment. We propose a novel multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression, combined with an anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement pipeline that eliminates drift-induced noise from training supervision. Together, these components enable a scaffold-free single-step diffusion completion model that achieves an approximately 16x reduction in squared Chamfer distance on SemanticKITTI seq. 08 (0.396 m^2 to 0.024 m^2), surpasses LiDiff and ScoreLiDAR by 17-19% and 10-11%, respectively, and operates at 25-143x lower inference latency. Our results demonstrate that data quality dominates model design in this regime and that multi-token latent spaces provide a stable first stage for latent diffusion-based scene completion.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling and anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression and single-step diffusion completion, achieving significant improvements in reconstruction quality (16x reduction in squared Chamfer distance) and 25-143x lower inference latency compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing latent diffusion approaches for sparse LiDAR completion suffer from unstable global representations at outdoor scale, corrupted ground truth data due to odometry drift, and prohibitive multi-step inference latency for real-time deployment.
AI.method: Multi-token Gaussian VAE with cross-attention pooling for stable scene-scale LiDAR compression + anchor-based ICP ground truth refinement to eliminate drift-induced noise + single-step diffusion completion model.
AI.result: 16x reduction in squared Chamfer distance on SemanticKITTI seq. 08 (0.396→0.024 m²), surpasses LiDiff by 17-19% and ScoreLiDAR by 10-11%, operates at 25-143x lower inference latency.
AI.conclusion: Data quality dominates model design; multi-token latent spaces provide a stable first stage for latent diffusion-based scene completion.

title: Stepwise Token Selection for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: In multimodal large language models (MLLMs), inference cost is largely dominated by the visual token prefix rather than the language backbone, making token reduction a key factor for improving efficiency. Existing approaches typically assign independent importance scores to visual tokens and retain a fixed number of top-ranked tokens, implicitly assuming token independence and a uniform compression ratio across inputs. In this work, we reformulate visual token pruning as a sequential decision-making process. Specifically, we introduce a pointer-style selection mechanism that iteratively chooses informative tokens, conditioning each decision on previously selected ones, and dynamically determines when to stop via a learned termination action. This enables joint optimization of both the selected subset and its size. To enable end-to-end training under standard language modeling objectives, we design a differentiable relaxation based on a variance-preserving noise interpolation scheme, allowing gradients to propagate through the discrete selection process. Extensive experiments on LLaVA-v1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms fixed-ratio baselines across different compression levels. Under aggressive pruning that removes 88.9% of visual tokens, our method preserves 94.6% of the original accuracy while achieving a 1.88x speed-up in prefill latency.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a sequential pointer-style selection mechanism for visual token pruning in MLLMs, dynamically deciding which tokens to keep and when to stop, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs (94.6% accuracy with 88.9% token removal and 1.88x speed-up).
AI.motivation: Inference cost in MLLMs is dominated by visual token prefix length; existing pruning methods assume token independence and fixed compression ratios, which is suboptimal.
AI.method: Reformulates visual token pruning as sequential decision-making with a pointer-style selection mechanism that iteratively selects tokens conditioned on previous choices, plus a learned termination action. Uses a differentiable relaxation via variance-preserving noise interpolation for end-to-end training.
AI.result: Outperforms fixed-ratio baselines on LLaVA-v1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B; under 88.9% token removal, preserves 94.6% original accuracy with 1.88x prefill speed-up.
AI.conclusion: Sequential, adaptive token pruning with learned termination is more effective than fixed-ratio methods, enabling aggressive compression with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Tool-IQA: Augmenting Image Quality Assessment with Simple Tools
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been increasingly adopted for Image Quality Assessment (IQA). However, current methods typically employ a static one-shot scoring paradigm, despite the fact that humans assess image quality through dynamic visual inspection, e.g., selectively adjusting views to verify details and subtle artifacts. Specifically, relying solely on a single-pass observation introduces two primary limitations: first, perceiving the image only at a global scale restricts the assessment of finer local details; second, the original intensity distribution of the image may overwhelm the visibility, leading to insufficient inspection of image quality. To address these issues, we propose Tool-IQA, shifting the assessment mechanism from passive scoring to a tool-augmented workflow. In particular, we equip VLMs with simple yet effective view tools: a Magnifier to inspect local details, and a Gamma Corrector to uncover visibility and hidden artifacts. The assessment follows a structured pipeline that consists of an initial observation with rubric notes, a tool-augmented in-depth inspection, and a final quantification for calibrated quality score. Furthermore, to ensure efficient and purposeful tool callings, we introduce a batch-aware training strategy to reward tool interactions that can yield positive contributions rather than simply encouraging usage. Experiments on a variety of IQA benchmarks demonstrate that, with effective tool calling and calibrated assessment, our proposed Tool-IQA significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models, e.g., it achieves a PLCC of 0.854 on the challenging CLIVE dataset.

AI.tldr: Tool-IQA improves Image Quality Assessment by equipping VLMs with view tools (Magnifier and Gamma Corrector) and a structured assessment pipeline, achieving SOTA results on IQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Humans assess image quality through dynamic visual inspection (adjusting views, verifying details), but current VLMs use static one-shot scoring, which limits assessment of fine local details and may overlook artifacts due to original intensity distributions.
AI.method: Proposes Tool-IQA with: (1) two view tools - Magnifier for local details and Gamma Corrector for uncovering hidden artifacts; (2) structured pipeline: initial observation with rubric notes → tool-augmented in-depth inspection → final calibrated score; (3) batch-aware training strategy to reward beneficial tool interactions.
AI.result: Outperforms existing SOTA models on multiple IQA benchmarks; achieves PLCC of 0.854 on the challenging CLIVE dataset.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from passive scoring to tool-augmented workflow with effective tool calling and calibrated assessment significantly improves VLM-based IQA performance.

title: VinQA: Visual Elements Interleaved Long-form Answer Generation for Real-World Multimodal Document QA
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Main paper: 5 figures, 4 tables; includes supplementary material
summary: Real-world documents combine text with tables, charts, photographs, and diagrams arranged in diverse layouts, yet existing research on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for document QA predominantly produces text-only responses, underutilizing these visual elements. We introduce VinQA, a dataset for long-form answer generation where cited visual elements are explicitly interleaved with their supporting text and grounded in relevant document pages. To support this task, we study two encoding methods for feeding raw document page images into an MLLM, along with their visual-element citation mechanisms: (1) Page Encoding, which directly encodes full-page images with bounding boxes of visual elements and treats these boxed regions as citable units; and (2) Modality Encoding, which parses each page to extract text and crop visual elements, encodes them separately, and uses these cropped elements as citable units. In our experiments, we propose M-GroSE, a multimodal evaluation framework extending GroUSE to assess answers along four dimensions: completeness, answer relevancy, faithfulness, and unanswerability. We additionally report Visual Source F1 to directly measure visual citation accuracy. Although proprietary frontier models still achieve the best overall scores on the VinQA test split, fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models on the training split substantially improves their performance and narrows this gap. Modality Encoding is initially more robust for complex documents with long text, many visual elements, and diverse citation requirements. After training on VinQA, however, Page Encoding reaches a comparable level, competing effectively even without the explicit parsing used in Modality Encoding. Finally, Visual G-Eval, an MLLM-based judge, confirms that fine-tuned models insert visual elements at semantically appropriate positions with faithful supporting text.

AI.tldr: VinQA introduces a dataset and evaluation framework (M-GroSE) for long-form document QA where answers interleave text with cited visual elements, comparing Page Encoding and Modality Encoding methods, showing that fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models on this dataset narrows the gap with proprietary frontier models.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal LLMs for document QA produce only text-only responses, failing to leverage tables, charts, and diagrams present in real-world documents. The authors aim to enable answers that explicitly cite and interleave visual elements with supporting text.
AI.method: The authors create the VinQA dataset and study two encoding methods: (1) Page Encoding (full-page images with bounding boxes) and (2) Modality Encoding (separately encoded text and cropped visual elements). They also propose M-GroSE, a multimodal evaluation framework assessing completeness, answer relevancy, faithfulness, and unanswerability, plus Visual Source F1 for citation accuracy.
AI.result: Proprietary frontier models achieve the best overall scores, but fine-tuning open Qwen2.5-VL models on VinQA substantially narrows this gap. Modality Encoding is initially more robust for complex documents, but Page Encoding reaches comparable levels after training. Fine-tuned models insert visual elements at semantically appropriate positions with faithful text.
AI.conclusion: The VinQA dataset and M-GroSE framework effectively enable and evaluate multimodal answers with cited visual elements, demonstrating that open models can be fine-tuned to compete with proprietary ones, and that both encoding methods are viable after training.

title: SceneCraft: Interactive System for Image Editing via Scene Graph
summary: Recent advances in generative AI have enabled natural language-driven image editing, yet existing systems often fail in complex scenes with multiple interacting objects because they rely heavily on users crafting precise text prompts. To address the absence of structured control, we propose SceneCraft, a novel interactive framework that bridges user intent and model execution by representing images as editable scene graphs. Instead of guessing text prompts through trial and error, users interact directly with a visual graph to perform complex spatial and relational operations. These graph modifications are automatically translated into precise, context-aware editing prompts, effectively eliminating linguistic ambiguity. To ensure robust and diverse results, structured prompts are dispatched to multiple state-of-the-art generative models. Evaluations across diverse editing scenarios show that SceneCraft provides a more intuitive control mechanism, significantly reducing the cognitive burden of manual prompt engineering while generating outputs that users consistently rate as higher in quality and fidelity.

AI.tldr: SceneCraft enables intuitive image editing through interactive scene graphs, eliminating the need for complex text prompts by translating visual graph modifications into precise editing instructions for multiple generative models.
AI.motivation: Existing generative AI image editing systems struggle with complex scenes containing multiple interacting objects and require users to craft precise text prompts through trial and error, creating a high cognitive burden and linguistic ambiguity.
AI.method: SceneCraft represents images as editable scene graphs, allowing users to directly interact with visual graph elements to perform spatial and relational operations. Graph modifications are automatically translated into context-aware editing prompts and dispatched to multiple state-of-the-art generative models.
AI.result: SceneCraft provides more intuitive control, significantly reduces cognitive burden of manual prompt engineering, and generates outputs that users consistently rate as higher in quality and fidelity across diverse editing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Scene graph-based interaction bridges user intent and model execution more effectively than text-prompt approaches, offering a structured and intuitive alternative for complex scene editing.

title: EdgeZSAD: Practical Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection on Edge Devices
summary: Industrial inspection needs zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) that remains useful under edge deployment constraints. Recent methods often rely on ViT-L foundation backbones (~300M parameters), which exceed the memory and operator budget of typical embedded hardware. We study this regime through EdgeZSAD, a compact reference system built around a TinyViT-21M-512 backbone, an asymmetric global-local readout (EdgeGLR), and a reproducible source-side training recipe (Real-IAD-DR). We train a single checkpoint in a source-trained, target-unseen protocol and evaluate it across six industrial benchmarks. Across three independent runs, the resulting model reaches an average image AUROC of 91.6 on MVTec-AD and 88.2 on VisA, while remaining directly deployable on Jetson Orin Nano Super (TensorRT FP16) and RB5 Gen2 (QNN GPU FP16). Across the six device-rescored benchmarks, image-AUROC drift stays below 0.2 points, indicating that the exported graph preserves host-side ranking behavior in the evaluated deployment setting.

AI.tldr: EdgeZSAD is a compact zero-shot anomaly detection system using TinyViT-21M backbone that achieves competitive performance (91.6 AUROC on MVTec-AD) while being deployable on edge devices like Jetson Orin Nano and RB5 Gen2.
AI.motivation: Existing ZSAD methods rely on large ViT-L backbones (~300M parameters) that exceed the memory and computational limits of typical embedded hardware used in industrial inspection.
AI.method: EdgeZSAD uses a TinyViT-21M-512 backbone, asymmetric global-local readout (EdgeGLR), and reproducible source-side training recipe (Real-IAD-DR) with a source-trained, target-unseen protocol.
AI.result: Achieves 91.6 image AUROC on MVTec-AD and 88.2 on VisA; deployable on Jetson Orin Nano (TensorRT FP16) and RB5 Gen2 (QNN GPU FP16); image-AUROC drift stays below 0.2 points across six device-rescored benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EdgeZSAD demonstrates that competitive zero-shot anomaly detection is achievable with compact models suitable for edge deployment, with minimal performance loss when exported to embedded hardware.

title: Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Visual Grounding for Remote Sensing Images and Videos
summary: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize a referred target in a remote sensing image or video according to a natural language expression. Existing RSVG methods usually rely on task-specific manual annotations, which are costly to collect and inevitably limited in covering the diversity of real-world geospatial scenarios. As a result, they often struggle to generalize to open-vocabulary queries involving novel objects, fine-grained attributes, complex spatial relationships, and functional semantics. In this paper, we propose RSVG-ZeroOV, a training-free framework that leverages frozen generic foundation models for zero-shot open-vocabulary RSVG. RSVG-ZeroOV follows an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm, which exploits the distinct yet complementary attention patterns of vision-language models (VLMs) and diffusion models (DMs) to progressively generate precise grounding results. Specifically, (i) Overview utilizes a VLM to extract cross-attention maps that capture semantic correlations between the referring expression and visual regions; (ii) Focus leverages the fine-grained modeling priors of a DM to compensate for object structure and shape information often overlooked by VLM attention; and (iii) Evolve introduces a simple yet effective attention evolution module to suppress irrelevant activations, yielding purified object masks. To handle video inputs, we further present Video RSVG-ZeroOV, which extends image-level grounding to spatio-temporal grounding through a query-relevant key-frame selector and a temporal propagator, enabling efficient and temporally coherent video grounding without video annotations or fine-tuning. Extensive experiments on six image and video grounding benchmarks show that RSVG-ZeroOV consistently outperforms existing zero-shot baselines and achieves competitive or superior performance compared with weakly- and fully-supervised methods.

AI.tldr: RSVG-ZeroOV is a training-free, zero-shot open-vocabulary framework for remote sensing visual grounding that uses frozen foundation models (VLMs and DMs) via an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm to localize objects in images/videos without task-specific annotations, outperforming zero-shot baselines and competing with supervised methods.
AI.motivation: Existing RSVG methods rely on costly task-specific manual annotations that limit coverage of diverse real-world geospatial scenarios, making them struggle to generalize to open-vocabulary queries involving novel objects, fine-grained attributes, complex spatial relationships, and functional semantics.
AI.method: The framework follows an Overview-Focus-Evolve paradigm: (1) Overview uses a VLM to extract cross-attention maps capturing semantic correlations; (2) Focus leverages a diffusion model's fine-grained priors to compensate for object structure/shape information; (3) Evolve introduces an attention evolution module to suppress irrelevant activations. For video, it adds a query-relevant key-frame selector and temporal propagator for spatio-temporal grounding.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six image and video grounding benchmarks show RSVG-ZeroOV consistently outperforms existing zero-shot baselines and achieves competitive/superior performance compared with weakly- and fully-supervised methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training-free framework effectively combines complementary strengths of VLMs and DMs for zero-shot open-vocabulary RSVG, eliminating the need for costly annotations while achieving strong performance across both image and video grounding tasks.

title: Shift-and-Sum Quantization for Visual Autoregressive Models
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) enables efficient deployment of deep networks using a small set of data. Its application to visual autoregressive models (VAR), however, remains relatively unexplored. We identify two key challenges for applying PTQ to VAR: (i) large reconstruction errors in attention-value products, especially at coarse scales where high attention scores occur more frequently; and (ii) a discrepancy between the sampling frequencies of codebook entries and their predicted probabilities due to limited calibration data. To address these challenges, we propose a PTQ framework tailored for VAR. First, we introduce a shift-and-sum quantization method that reduces reconstruction errors by aggregating quantized results from symmetrically shifted duplicates of value tokens. Second, we present a resampling strategy for calibration data that aligns sampling frequencies of codebook entries with their predicted probabilities. Experiments on class-conditional image generation, inpainting, outpainting, and class-conditional editing show consistent improvements across VAR architectures, establishing a new state of the art in PTQ for VAR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel Post-training Quantization (PTQ) framework for Visual Autoregressive Models (VAR), addressing attention-value reconstruction errors via shift-and-sum quantization and calibration data distribution mismatch via a resampling strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: PTQ for VAR is underexplored, and existing methods face two challenges: (1) large reconstruction errors in attention-value products at coarse scales where high attention scores occur frequently; (2) discrepancy between codebook entry sampling frequencies and their predicted probabilities due to limited calibration data.
AI.method: (1) Shift-and-sum quantization: reduces reconstruction errors by aggregating quantized results from symmetrically shifted duplicates of value tokens. (2) Resampling strategy for calibration data: aligns sampling frequencies of codebook entries with their predicted probabilities.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across VAR architectures on class-conditional image generation, inpainting, outpainting, and class-conditional editing tasks, establishing a new state of the art in PTQ for VAR.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PTQ framework effectively addresses the unique challenges of quantizing VAR models, enabling efficient deployment without significant performance degradation.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Medical Image Segmentation: Challenges, Benchmarks, and Beyond
comment: 12 pages,3 figures,1 table. All related resources are available at https://github.com/andrew-pengyu/Awsome_MedSeg/tree/main
summary: Medical image segmentation plays a critical role in clinical diagnostics, treatment planning, disease monitoring, and neurological disorder identification. This article presents a comprehensive review of its systematic development, covering widely used public datasets, representative methods built on the U-Net, Transformer, and SAM architectures, and key evaluation metrics with their differences, followed by an analysis of major challenges from multiple perspectives. Unlike surveys that focus on a single model family or a specific clinical application, this review organizes U-Net-, Transformer-, and SAM-based methods within a unified analytical framework, with a particular focus on their effectiveness in improving segmentation accuracy and efficiency. This work aims to guide future research and support clinical translation of medical image segmentation, with all related resources publicly available in our GitHub repository: https://github.com/andrew-pengyu/Awsome_MedSeg/tree/main.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of medical image segmentation covering U-Net, Transformer, and SAM architectures, datasets, evaluation metrics, and challenges, with a unified analytical framework and public GitHub resources.
AI.motivation: To provide a systematic, unified review of medical image segmentation methods across multiple architectures (U-Net, Transformer, SAM) rather than focusing on a single model family or specific clinical application, guiding future research and clinical translation.
AI.method: Organizes U-Net-, Transformer-, and SAM-based methods within a unified analytical framework; reviews public datasets, representative methods, and evaluation metrics; analyzes major challenges from multiple perspectives.
AI.result: A structured taxonomy and comparative analysis of segmentation methods across three major architectural families, highlighting their effectiveness in improving segmentation accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: This review serves as a guide for future research and supports clinical translation of medical image segmentation, with all related resources publicly available on GitHub.

title: Focus When Necessary: Adaptive Routing and Collaborative Grounding for Training-Free Visual Grounding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in cross-modal reasoning, they often struggle to perceive fine-grained details in complex high-resolution images.
